Two etymological/morphological interpretations reinforce divergent senses. 4) cleave (classic contronym) - Sense A: split - Sense B: cling Not procedural per se, but illustrative: historical convergence of two different Old English verbs into one form. 5) fast - Sense A: quick - Sense B: fixed/firm (hold fast) Mechanism: different roots/semantic developments; “fast” = firm → “fast” movement via “firmly/securely”? (Complex history, but the coexistence yields opposite intuitions.) 6) clip - Sense A: attach (“clip on”) - Sense B: cut off (“clip off”) Institutional/technical: common in instructions; the object (hair, coupon, device) disambiguates. 7) screen - Sense A: show/display (screen a film) - Sense B: conceal/protect (a screen blocks view) Institutional shorthand: “screening” in medicine = test; in security = filter; in film = show. One term for multiple gatekeeping/display functions. 8) audit (near-contronym in business discourse) - Sense A: examine/check carefully - Sense B (colloquial student sense): attend a class without credit (audit a course) Institutional driver: university policy specialized meaning; general sense remains “inspect.” 9) review (near-contronym in academia/law) - Sense A: evaluate critically (peer review) - Sense B: look again to refresh (review for an exam) Procedural shorthand: different workflows use “review” for either evaluation or repetition. 10) clemency / pardon / commute (procedural confusables) Not contronyms but procedural near-contrasts often blurred in public discourse; institutions define them sharply, but everyday use collapses them. How shorthand creates ambiguity - Step-labeling: institutions name steps (“lay on the table,” “call the question,” “move to reconsider”). Shortened labels survive while the original step context is forgotten. - Polysemy under one “official act” umbrella: words like sanction, execute, endorse can cover both enabling and constraining actions, because both are “acts of authority.” - Morphological reanalysis: oversight shows how speakers reinterpret parts of a word, supporting divergent senses. - Domain collision: screen in film vs security vs medicine—each domain reuses the same metaphor (filtering/mediating) but flips the “direction” (letting through vs blocking). Want a deeper dive into one domain—parliamentary procedure, legal terms, or corporate governance language?
Corporate governance language. I’m curious how terms like “fiduciary,” “material,” “consideration,” “equity,” “security,” “chair,” and “minutes” ended up with these specialized meanings.

Great set—corporate governance is a semantic-change playground because it mixes Latin/French legal inheritances with metonymy, narrowing, and institutional stabilization. 1) fiduciary - Source: Latin fiducia “trust, confidence,” from fidere “to trust.” - Path: general “trust” concept → Roman/medieval legal notion of holding something in trust → modern legal category: a person/entity with duties of loyalty and care toward another (beneficiary, shareholders, client). - Mechanisms: narrowing + technicalization. Institutions (courts, statutes) define and stabilize it; everyday speech rarely uses it outside legal/finance contexts. 2) material (as in “material fact,” “material breach”) - Source: Latin materia “matter, substance.” - Early senses: “physical substance” → “relevant matter.” - Legal/business specialized sense: “significant enough to affect a decision/outcome.” - Mechanisms: metaphor (matter/substance → importance), then institutional narrowing (relevance threshold becomes a test). The specialized sense is not just “important,” but “outcome-affecting under a standard.” 3) consideration (contract law) - Source: Latin considerare “to examine, contemplate.” - Early senses: “thoughtful regard; deliberation.” - Legal sense: the bargained-for exchange (something of value) required for enforceable contracts (in common law). - Mechanisms: narrowing + institutional redefinition. Courts took a general deliberation word and pinned it to a doctrinal requirement. This is one of the clearest “common word → doctrine term” shifts. 4) equity - Source: Latin aequitas “fairness, evenness.” - Early senses: “fairness; impartiality.” - Institutional split: in English legal history, Equity becomes a parallel body of law (Court of Chancery) addressing remedies not available at common law. - Modern corporate/finance senses: - “equity” = ownership interest (shareholders’ equity). - “equity” = fairness/justice (still). - Mechanisms: institutionalization (a legal system named Equity) + metonymy (the legal concept → the body of law) + later technical accounting sense (residual interest). Polysemy persists because the moral root still feels intuitive. 5) security - Source: Latin securus “free from care” (se- “without” + cura “care”). - Early senses: “safety; freedom from anxiety.” - Legal/financial sense: “collateral/pledge” and later “tradable financial instrument” (securities). - Mechanisms: semantic shift via metonymy: the state of being secure → the means that makes one secure (collateral) → institutional broadening to instruments that “secure” investment/obligation. Then plural “securities” becomes a technical category. 6) chair (governance) - Source: Latin cathedra “seat,” via Old French chaiere/chaire. - Path: physical seat → the person occupying a seat of authority (the chair of a meeting; chairperson). - Mechanism: metonymy (object → role). Institutions formalize it in bylaws and procedure. 7) minutes (meeting minutes) - Source: Latin minuta “small things,” feminine of minutus “small,” used in Medieval Latin for “minute” units; also “minute” as a unit of time is from the same “small division” idea. - Path: “small items/notes” → “brief notes of proceedings” → formal record. - Mechanisms: narrowing + institutional stabilization. “Minutes” are not just any notes; they’re an official record with procedural/legal weight. Bonus: board - Old English bord “plank, table.” - Path: table where people meet → the group that meets at the table (board of directors). - Mechanism: metonymy; classic governance vocabulary. These are good examples of how institutions create “semantic gravity wells”: once a word is used in statutes, case law, bylaws, and standardized documents, its technical sense becomes durable, even if everyday speech keeps other senses alive. If you want, I can do mini “false friend” warnings: e.g., “material” in everyday speech vs “material” in securities law; “consideration” in politeness vs in contract doctrine.
Please do the “false friend” warnings, and add “ordinary,” “reasonable,” and “constructive” as legal adjectives that mean something more specific than people think.

fiduciary - Everyday intuition: “trustworthy person.” - Legal/technical: a role with enforceable duties (loyalty, care, sometimes disclosure), conflicts rules, and remedies. Not just “I trust you,” but a structured relationship recognized by law. - Pitfall: calling someone a “fiduciary” loosely can imply legal obligations that may not exist. material - Everyday: “important.” - Legal: “significant under a defined test,” often “a reasonable person would consider it important” or “would affect the total mix of information.” Context-dependent threshold. - Pitfall: “material” isn’t “anywhat important”; it’s “important enough under doctrine.” consideration - Everyday: “thoughtfulness; politeness; taking into account.” - Contract law: the bargained-for exchange (something of value) that makes a promise enforceable in common law systems. - Pitfall: “I’ll do it out of consideration” is not “consideration” legally unless it’s part of the bargain. equity - Everyday: “fairness.” - Legal: a body of principles/remedies (injunctions, specific performance) historically separate from common law; also a set of doctrines. - Finance/accounting: residual ownership interest. - Pitfall: “equity” in a governance context may mean ownership stake, not fairness. security / securities - Everyday: safety; protection. - Finance: instruments like stocks, bonds, derivatives (jurisdiction-specific definitions). - Pitfall: “security” can also mean collateral; and “security” in IT/security is yet another specialized domain. minutes - Everyday: time units. - Governance: official record of meeting proceedings. - Pitfall: “minutes” are often discoverable in litigation; informal tone can have consequences. ordinary (legal) - Everyday: normal, unremarkable. - Legal: often means “regular” as opposed to “extraordinary,” and can be tied to standards (ordinary course of business). It’s not just “boring”—it’s a classification with implications. - Pitfall: “ordinary course” can exclude one-off transactions even if they seem “normal” to a layperson. reasonable (legal) - Everyday: sensible. - Legal: tied to the “reasonable person” standard, foreseeability, proportionality, and context; often an objective test, not what any particular person thinks is sensible. - Pitfall: “reasonable” is not “what I feel is fair”; it’s what the standard would deem reasonable given facts. constructive (legal) - Everyday: helpful (constructive criticism). - Legal: “imputed by law” rather than actual: constructive notice, constructive possession, constructive dismissal. - Mechanism: metaphor-ish but really technicalization: “constructed” by legal reasoning. - Pitfall: “constructive” in legal phrases often signals a legal fiction or imputation. If you want to see semantic drift interacting with doctrine, “constructive” is a perfect example: it’s not drift in the casual sense; it’s deliberate technical meaning that looks familiar but behaves differently.
Now zoom out. Could you outline a taxonomy of semantic change types, but with subtypes and diagnostics? Like, how do we tell metaphor from metonymy, bleaching from broadening, etc.?

Taxonomy of semantic change (with subtypes + diagnostics) I) Extension/Restriction of denotation A) Broadening (generalization) - Definition: word applies to a wider set of referents. - Diagnostic: earlier usage requires additional features; later usage drops constraints. - Example: holiday “holy day” → “vacation/day off.” - Pitfall: broadening can co-occur with bleaching. B) Narrowing (specialization) - Diagnostic: later usage adds constraints (only a subset of earlier referents). - Example: meat “food” → “animal flesh.” C) Semantic shift (neither strictly broader nor narrower) - Diagnostic: referent set changes category; overlaps partially. - Example: bead “prayer” → “small perforated ball” (via rosary association; could be metonymy-driven). II) Evaluative change A) Pejoration - Diagnostic: affective valence becomes more negative over time. - Example: vulgar “common” → “coarse.” B) Amelioration - Example: knight “servant” → “noble warrior.” III) Figurative transfer (source-target mapping) A) Metaphor (similarity-based) - Diagnostic: mapping across conceptual domains based on perceived resemblance (often physical → mental; concrete → abstract). - Tests: 1) Does the shift preserve relational structure via analogy? (grasp: hand action → mental understanding) 2) Can you paraphrase as “X is like Y”? - Example: bright “shiny” → “intelligent.” B) Metonymy (contiguity-based) - Diagnostic: shift within same domain via association (part-whole, container-contained, institution-place). - Tests: 1) Can you paraphrase as “X is associated with Y” rather than “like Y”? 2) Often involves real-world adjacency: crown (object) → monarchy (institution). - Examples: tongue (organ) → language; board (table) → committee. C) Synecdoche (often treated as subtype of metonymy) - Part-for-whole or whole-for-part. - Example: hands “workers” (part for whole). IV) Pragmatic/inferential change A) Subjectification / intersubjectification - Meaning shifts toward speaker attitude or discourse management. - Diagnostic: later meaning encodes stance (“honestly,” “really,” “just”). - Example: really from “in reality” → intensifier/discourse marker. B) Conventionalization of implicature - A conversational inference becomes lexical meaning. - Diagnostic: the inferred meaning becomes available without the original context. - Example-ish: “I’m sorry” from “I feel sorrow” → apology formula (not exactly semantic drift of a content word, but pragmatic conventionalization). V) Semantic bleaching (weakening) - Definition: loss of specific descriptive content; often becomes grammatical/functional. - Diagnostic: 1) Reduced selectional restrictions (“very” can modify almost anything gradable). 2) Less literal truth-conditional contribution; more scalar/intensifying role. - Example: literally used as intensifier in some registers. Distinguishing bleaching vs broadening - Broadening: extension of referents while core descriptive content still matters. - Bleaching: the content itself thins; the word becomes more “operator-like.” Example: - broadcast broadens from radio/TV to any dissemination (broadening). - very bleaches from “true” to intensifier (bleaching). VI) Grammaticalization / lexicalization (boundary with semantics) - Grammaticalization: lexical item becomes grammatical marker (going to → gonna future). - Lexicalization: phrase becomes fixed lexical item (by and large). Diagnostics include phonological reduction, obligatoriness, and syntactic behavior changes. VII) Institutional technicalization (special subtype) - Words acquire precise meanings in law/science/bureaucracy. - Diagnostic: definitions in statutes/standards; stable specialized collocations; divergence from everyday sense. - Examples: consideration (contract), material (securities), assault (criminal law definitions). If you want, we can take a handful of ambiguous cases (e.g., “bead,” “broadcast,” “virus” in computing, “cloud”) and run the diagnostics explicitly: is it metaphor, metonymy, or institutional technicalization with metaphorical motivation?
Run the diagnostics on “virus,” “cloud,” “meme,” “bead,” and “screen.” I want you to label each shift and justify it.

virus - Baseline sense: biological pathogen (Latin virus “poison,” though modern biological sense is later scientific). - Shift: “computer virus” (self-replicating malicious code). - Label: metaphor with some metonymic reinforcement. - Metaphor diagnostic: cross-domain mapping from biology → computing based on similarity of behavior (infection, replication, spread, host). - Not mere contiguity; it’s analogy-driven (“this code behaves like a virus”). - Institutional reinforcement: cybersecurity discourse standardizes the metaphor into technical vocabulary. cloud - Baseline: visible mass of condensed water vapor in sky. - Shift: “the cloud” in computing (remote servers/services). - Label: metaphor + institutional branding/technicalization. - Metaphor: something amorphous, distributed, not tied to a single visible location; also diagrammatic metaphor (networks drawn as cloud shapes). - Diagnostics: “X is like a cloud” (vague boundary, location-indeterminate) fits; plus a specific visual convention in engineering diagrams is a driver. - Institutional: industry marketing and standards stabilize “cloud” as a term. meme - Baseline: coined by Dawkins (1976) for a unit of cultural transmission (analogy to gene). - Shift: internet meme (a replicating template/joke/image format). - Label: narrowing + re-specialization + metaphor already built in. - Original is already metaphorical (gene analogy). - Internet sense is a subtype (narrowing) of cultural transmissible unit, but with strong prototypical features (digital, remixable, virality). - Diagnostics: later usage often excludes non-internet cultural units, suggesting specialization rather than broadening. bead - Earlier sense in English: “prayer” (Old English bēd “prayer”; related to bid). - Shift: “bead” as small perforated ball (rosary bead). - Label: metonymy (contiguity/association) + narrowing. - Metonymy diagnostic: prayer is associated with rosary counting objects used during prayers; object linked to the activity. - Path: “prayer” → “prayer-counting object” → “small perforated decorative object” (broadening after that within object category). - Not metaphor: the bead isn’t “like” a prayer; it’s used in praying. screen - Baseline: barrier/shield that conceals or protects (Old French escren). - Shifts: 1) “screen” as a partition; then “projection screen” (surface). 2) “screen a film” (show on a screen). 3) “screen applicants/patients” (filter/test). - Labels: - Barrier → projection surface: metonymy/functional shift (same artifact category; a screen can be a surface that mediates visibility). - Projection surface → show a film: metonymy (place/instrument → activity). - Barrier → filter/test: metaphor + metonymy hybrid: screening as “filtering out” (metaphor from physical screening/sifting) and institutional specialization in medicine/security/HR. Diagnostics: - “associated with” test fits for “screen a film” (using a screen). - “like” test fits for screening applicants (filtering people like filtering particles). These are great because they show different engines: bead is textbook metonymy; virus and cloud are metaphor stabilized by institutions; screen is polysemy built from multiple small steps.
Can we talk about semantic drift in intensifiers? Like “awfully,” “terribly,” “wicked,” “hella.” How do negative words become boosters?

This is a well-studied pathway: negative or extreme-evaluative adjectives/adverbs become degree modifiers (intensifiers). The usual story involves hyperbole, affect, and bleaching. Core mechanism chain (common pattern) 1) Source meaning: strong evaluation (often negative, fear/terror, moral judgment). 2) Hyperbolic use: speaker uses it to express intensity rather than literal evaluation. 3) Bleaching: the negative content weakens; the word becomes a general booster. 4) Distribution expansion: it attaches to more adjective types, including positive ones. 5) Sometimes: re-pejoration in some contexts or stigma as “informal.” Examples - terribly - Then: “in a terrible manner; causing terror.” - Bridge: “terribly sorry” (intensity of emotion). - Now: “very” (terribly good, terribly nice) in many varieties, though style guides may still flag it as informal. - awfully - Then: “in a way that inspires awe/dread.” - Now: “very” (awfully kind). The dread component is mostly gone. - dreadfully - Similar path; often keeps a slightly negative coloring, but can intensify positives in some registers. - wicked (regional, e.g., New England) - Then: morally bad. - Now: “very” (wicked smart). Bleaching + in-group identity marker. - hella (West Coast US origins; debated etymology often from “hell of a”) - Path: “hell of a” (strong colloquial intensifier) → hella as adverb/determiner (“hella cool,” “hella people”). - Mechanisms: clipping + grammaticalization-like behavior (new syntactic distributions). Why negatives are fertile intensifiers - Extremity bias: words for fear, evil, disaster are already “high on the scale,” so they’re good raw material for “very.” - Emotional salience: negative terms carry force; repeated hyperbole bleaches literal content. - Polarity flexibility: once reanalyzed as degree markers, they can modify positives because they encode magnitude, not valence. Diagnostics for “has it become an intensifier?” - Can it modify a positive adjective naturally? (terribly good) - Can it appear in positions typical for degree adverbs? (so/very/quite slot) - Does it contribute truth-conditional negativity, or just intensity? - Does it allow comparative/stacking behavior? (less common, but you can test “so terribly” etc.) If you want, I can compare “very/really/literally” (more discourse-operator-ish) versus “awfully/terribly” (still somewhat lexical) and show how they differ in syntactic constraints and register.
Yes, compare those two clusters, including syntactic behavior. And toss in “pretty” as an intensifier too, since that one feels weird.

Cluster 1: “operator-ish” intensifiers (very, really, literally in intensifier use) Traits: - High frequency, broad collocational range. - Weak descriptive content (bleached). - Often participate in discourse functions (stance, emphasis, turn-taking). very - History: from “true” → degree adverb. - Syntax: typically modifies gradable adjectives/adverbs (very tall, very quickly), not usually nouns (*very a problem). - Constraints: prefers gradable predicates; awkward with absolute adjectives unless coerced (very dead is jocular/marked). - Register: neutral. really - Two lives: 1) Epistemic adverb: “in reality” (really happened). 2) Degree/stance: “really good,” plus discourse “Really?” expressing surprise/skepticism. - Syntax: more flexible than very; can modify verbs in some uses (I really like it) where it shades into stance/intensity. - Register: informal-to-neutral. literally (in intensifier/hyperbole use) - Competing norms: prescriptive resistance because it clashes with “literal.” - Syntax: can modify clauses/verbs/adjectives; often signals emphasis rather than degree alone. - Diagnostics: truth-conditional content is often weakened; it can function as a pragmatic marker (“I literally died” = “I felt like I died”). - Register: informal; socially indexed. Cluster 2: “lexical-source” intensifiers (awfully, terribly, dreadfully, horribly, insanely, wicked) Traits: - Retain some “echo” of source meaning (often stylistic coloring). - More restricted to certain registers/regions. - Often feel hyperbolic or expressive. awfully/terribly/horribly - Syntax: degree adverbs modifying adjectives/adverbs; also can modify verbs (I awfully regret…) but that’s more archaic/formal. - Collocations: often with evaluative adjectives (awfully sorry, terribly upset), but can extend to positives (terribly nice). - Coloring: “awfully” can sound quaint or British; “horribly” retains a faint negative vibe. wicked (regional intensifier) - Syntax: typically pre-adjective (wicked smart). Less common with adverbs (*wicked quickly is possible but regionally marked). - Strong social indexicality: signals in-group dialect. pretty (as an intensifier) - Origin: adjective meaning “clever/skillful” → “attractive” (amelioration and shift). - Intensifier use: “pretty good,” “pretty big.” - Why it feels weird: the adjective “pretty” (attractive) seems unrelated, but the intensifier pretty likely comes through an older adverbial sense meaning “in a pretty manner” → “to a considerable degree” (a common adverbial drift: “fairly” did something similar). - Syntax: modifies gradable adjectives; tends to imply “moderately” rather than “extremely” (pretty good = fairly good). That scalar profile is part of its conventional meaning now. - Diagnostics: if it were just “attractive,” it wouldn’t fit; but as a degree adverb it has stable scalar semantics. Quick syntactic contrasts (useful diagnostics) - Verb modification: - really: common (I really like it). - very: rare/marked (*I very like it). - terribly/awfully: possible but formal/rare (I terribly regret…). - Noun modification: - hella: can do it in some dialects (hella people). - very/really/pretty: generally no (*very people). - Degree stacking: - really very good (possible). - very really good (odd). This hints at different scopes and discourse roles. If you want a deeper technical angle, we can talk about “degree semantics”: which intensifiers target the adjective’s degree argument vs operate as speaker-oriented emphasis, and how that correlates with syntactic distribution.
Let’s go deeper: degree semantics. Explain the difference between maximizing vs moderating intensifiers, and how conventional scalar meaning emerges.

Degree semantics in a nutshell: many adjectives are gradable—they map an entity to a position on a scale (tallness, goodness, loudness). Degree modifiers manipulate where on that scale you’re placing something, or they adjust the standard of comparison. 1) Maximizers vs boosters vs moderators vs diminishers A) Maximizers (approach the top of a scale) - Examples: completely, totally, absolutely (with compatible adjectives). - Semantics: set degree to the maximum or to the endpoint; often require “closed-scale” adjectives (full/empty, finished, dead) though colloquial use can coerce them. - Diagnostics: “completely full” is natural; “completely tall” is odd unless reinterpreted. B) Boosters (increase degree, but not necessarily to maximum) - Examples: very, really, so, extremely, terribly. - Semantics: raise degree above a contextual standard; doesn’t imply endpoint. - Diagnostics: works well with open-scale adjectives (tall, interesting). C) Moderators (raise slightly / indicate moderate degree) - Examples: pretty, fairly, rather, quite (BrE often), somewhat (more like a downtoner but can be mid-scale). - Semantics: place degree above standard but not extremely; often implicates “not as much as you might think.” - Diagnostics: “pretty good” often implies “good but not amazing.” D) Diminishers/downtoners - Examples: slightly, a bit, kind of, sort of. - Semantics: reduce commitment to high degree; can also hedge category membership (kind of blue). 2) How conventional scalar meaning emerges (from drift) A word starts as lexical content, then gets used repeatedly in contexts where a degree inference is salient. Over time, that inference becomes conventional. Example pathway (terribly) - Stage 1: literal: “in a terrifying way.” - Stage 2: affective hyperbole: “terribly sorry” (the “terror” isn’t literal; intensity of emotion is). - Stage 3: bleaching: “terribly” contributes “high degree” without terror. - Stage 4: scalar conventionalization: speakers learn “terribly” as a booster with a certain register flavor. For moderators like pretty - Stage 1: adverbial “prettily” meaning “in a pleasing way” existed; “pretty” as an adverb develops in English (historically many adjectives served adverbially). - Stage 2: “pretty” used as a general modifier meaning “considerably” (already a degree use). - Stage 3: conventional moderation: in many dialects, “pretty” settles into a mid-high but not extreme zone. That’s not logical necessity; it’s conventionalized scalar strength. 3) Diagnostics for “how strong is it?” - Entailment tests: does “X is absolutely A” entail “X is A”? Usually yes. Does “X is pretty A” entail “X is A”? Generally yes, but with hedging contexts it can get squishy. - Compatibility with endpoints: - absolutely/completely fit endpoints. - very/pretty fit open scales. - Scalar implicatures: - “pretty good” often implicates “not excellent.” - “very good” doesn’t carry the same “not excellent” implication as strongly. 4) Why different words settle at different strengths - Frequency and competition: very became the default booster; others differentiate by register or strength. - Social indexicality: wicked, hella become identity markers; their strength can inflate in-group. - Prescriptive pressure: literally’s contested status can push it toward expressive emphasis rather than pure degree. If you want, we can do a mini “scale placement” chart for a set of intensifiers in a given dialect (pretty < very < really < extremely < absolutely), with caveats about context and adjective type.
Okay, chart it. Use a few adjective types: open-scale (tall), closed-scale (full), evaluative (good), and emotive (sorry). Show which intensifiers feel natural with each and why.

Here’s a compact “compatibility and typical strength” chart. Think of it as tendencies, not hard rules—context can coerce odd pairings. Intensifiers to compare: - Moderator: pretty - Default booster: very - Booster/stance: really - High booster: extremely - Maximizer: absolutely / completely - Lexical-source booster: terribly Adjective types: A) Open-scale: tall - pretty tall: natural; suggests moderately high. - very tall: natural; strong booster. - really tall: natural; similar to very, sometimes more conversational. - extremely tall: natural; very high degree. - absolutely/completely tall: odd (no clear endpoint for tallness). - terribly tall: possible but marked; feels humorous or old-fashioned unless in a context where “terribly” is just a booster. Why: tall is open-scale; maximizers prefer closed scales. B) Closed-scale: full (endpoint “full”) - pretty full: natural; implies near full but not necessarily at endpoint. - very full: natural; implies high degree but may not guarantee endpoint. - really full: natural; similar to very. - extremely full: natural-ish; implies close to endpoint. - completely full: very natural; hits endpoint reading. - absolutely full: natural; emphatic endpoint. - terribly full: possible but expressive; often implies discomfort rather than degree literalness. Why: closed-scale adjectives invite maximizers because “complete” maps to endpoint. C) Evaluative: good - pretty good: very common; often implies “good, not amazing.” - very good: strong positive. - really good: strong, conversational; can also signal speaker stance. - extremely good: sounds more formal/analytic; strong. - absolutely good: odd unless “good” is coerced into an absolute/moral category; “absolutely” tends to want an endpoint-like reading. - completely good: odd. - terribly good: dialect/register dependent; can be quaint (“terribly good show”) or just booster. Why: “good” is gradable but not naturally endpointed; maximizers are less compatible unless “good” is treated as categorical. D) Emotive predicate: sorry - pretty sorry: can mean “somewhat apologetic” or “in poor condition” depending on sense; ambiguity. - very sorry: standard apology intensification. - really sorry: very common in speech; strong sincerity signal. - extremely sorry: formal, sometimes overdone. - absolutely sorry: odd; but “absolutely” can work as a discourse emphatic (“I’m absolutely sorry”) though it may feel stiff. - completely sorry: odd. - terribly sorry: classic; historically one of the bridge contexts for terribly as intensifier. Why: “sorry” in apology contexts is already about speaker attitude; boosters like really/terribly fit because they intensify affect, not a measurable property. Takeaway rule of thumb: - Open-scale adjectives: prefer boosters/moderators (pretty/very/really/extremely). - Closed-scale adjectives: allow maximizers (completely/absolutely) naturally. - Emotive/stance adjectives: welcome boosters that also function pragmatically (really/terribly), because they encode sincerity/emphasis. If you want, we can add “quite” and show the BrE/AmE split, because it behaves like a moderator vs maximizer depending on dialect and adjective class.
Yes, “quite” and “rather.” And include the BrE adjective-class effect you mentioned earlier.

quite - Core issue: in BrE, quite is sensitive to adjective type (“scalar” vs “absolute”), producing different default strengths. In AmE, quite often leans more uniformly toward “very/completely,” though it still varies. BrE adjective-class effect (common generalization) 1) With gradable/scalar adjectives (open-scale): quite often ≈ “fairly/rather” - quite tall = moderately tall - quite good = fairly good This makes quite a moderator/weak booster. 2) With non-gradable/absolute or “limit” adjectives (closed-scale / categorical): quite often ≈ “completely/entirely” - quite certain = completely certain - quite impossible = completely impossible - quite dead (rare but in principle) = completely dead Here quite behaves more like a maximizer. Why: If the adjective is treated as having an endpoint (certain/impossible), “quite” can naturally mean “to the full extent.” With open scales, it defaults to “to a considerable extent” (moderate). AmE tendency - quite good often ≈ “very good” (stronger than BrE). - quite certain can still be “completely certain,” similar to BrE. So AmE shifts the scalar-adjective reading upward in strength. rather - In BrE: often a moderator with a slightly formal or dry tone; can also convey mild negative evaluation or surprise. - rather tall = somewhat tall (often implies “more than expected”) - rather good = fairly good, sometimes with an understated positive. - In AmE: rather exists but can sound formal/posh; “pretty” and “somewhat” often cover its territory. Diagnostics / pragmatic coloring - Understatement: BrE quite/rather can be used for litotes-like understatement (“quite interesting” meaning “very interesting” in context, but conventionally it’s a mild booster—context supplies irony). - Prosody matters: “QUITE good” vs “quite GOOD” can flip perceived strength and attitude. - Polarity: rather can carry a faint “not ideal” flavor (“rather late” = inconveniently late). Mini-compatibility examples (BrE-ish defaults) - quite tall (moderate) - quite full (near full; could be close to endpoint but not necessarily) - quite certain (maximizer-ish) - rather tall (moderate, possibly “more than expected”) - rather full (moderate-high) - rather sorry (can be apology intensifier but also “somewhat regretful”) So “quite” is a great example of how degree modifiers can develop multi-modal scalar semantics conditioned by adjective class, and how dialect conventionalization changes the default strength.

This is exactly my jam. One more thing: can you give me a compact “field guide” to spotting semantic drift in the wild when reading older texts? Like what to watch for so I don’t misread Shakespeare or Austen.

Field guide to semantic drift (practical heuristics) 1) Flag “everyday” words that look too easy High-risk items are common words with long histories: nice, presently, prevent, silly, fond, jealous, artificial, curious, ordinary, sensible, incredible. If an old sentence feels oddly blunt or oddly mild, suspect drift. 2) Watch for false-friend adverbs and connectives - presently: often “soon” in older BrE; can mislead as “now.” - anon: “soon/at once.” - by and by: “before long” rather than “next to.” 3) Check moral/mental-state vocabulary Older English often uses words like: - jealous = “zealous/vigilant” (in some contexts). - melancholy, humour, passion: tied to older psychological models. If a character’s trait seems inconsistent, the word may have shifted. 4) Note institutional context cues If the text is about law, church, or social rank, expect technical or archaic senses: - solicitor, attorney, parson, curate, living (as a church benefice), preferment. - villain/churl/knave can be status terms shading into insult. 5) Look for collocations that “don’t fit” modern meaning Words travel with typical partners. If you see: - “prevent” + “him” (prevent him = “anticipate him” / “go before him” in older usage) - “silly” + “lamb” (innocent/helpless) Collocation mismatch is a strong drift signal. 6) Beware of “compliment” words that used to be neutral or negative - cunning = skilled/knowing. - artificial = artful/skillful. - awful = awe-inspiring. - vulgar = common. 7) Track whether a word is gradable now but wasn’t (or vice versa) Degree modification can reveal older senses. If an adjective is intensified in a way that seems off, it may have had different scalar properties. 8) Use paraphrase tests Try substituting a near-synonym appropriate to the era: - If “nice” appears, try “precise/fastidious” before “pleasant.” - If “resent” appears, consider “feel” (resent once meant “feel” in older English/French influence). 9) Recognize register traps Some words keep older senses in fixed phrases: - trespass (religious) - intercourse (older “communication” sense survives in historical contexts) - ejaculation (older “exclamation” sense in some texts) 10) When in doubt: triangulate - Look up the word in an historical dictionary (OED is the gold standard). - Check multiple citations around the same date. - Look for parallel usage in the author’s other works. If you want, name a short excerpt (a few lines) from Shakespeare/Austen/any older text, and I can do a “drift-aware” close reading: identify likely false friends, propose period-appropriate paraphrases, and explain the mechanisms behind each.
I keep mixing up indicative and subjunctive in Spanish. Is there a simple rule for when to use the subjunctive? I’m honestly getting sick of guessing.

A workable “simple rule” is: use the subjunctive when the speaker is not asserting the clause as a fact, but as something uncertain, desired, feared, denied, evaluated, or dependent on someone’s will or opinion. Use the indicative when you’re presenting information as real/known/claimed as true. That’s the big idea. But since Spanish doesn’t label it for you, it helps to learn the main triggers in groups: 1) Volition/Influence: querer que, pedir que, recomendar que, insistir en que → subjunctive. 2) Emotion/value judgment: alegrarse de que, temer que, es bueno que → subjunctive. 3) Doubt/denial/uncertainty: dudar que, no creer que, no es seguro que → subjunctive. 4) Impersonal expressions: es posible que, es probable que, es una lástima que → subjunctive (vs es evidente que → indicative). 5) Certain time/condition clauses about the future: cuando, en cuanto, hasta que, después de que (future) → subjunctive. 6) Relative clauses with non-specific antecedents: busco a alguien que sepa… → subjunctive. If you tell me a few sentences you’ve been guessing on, I can show you why each one takes indicative vs subjunctive.

Fine. Here are the ones that keep tripping me: “Creo que ella viene/venga mañana.” “No creo que él es/sea honesto.” “Es obvio que tengas/tienes razón.” And “Te llamo cuando llego/llegue.” I always pick the wrong one.

Good set—these hit the core contrasts. 1) Creo que ella viene mañana. (indicative) “Creo que…” asserts belief; you’re presenting “she’s coming tomorrow” as what you take to be true. Subjunctive would sound like you’re not asserting it: - No creo que ella venga mañana. (subjunctive, because you’re denying belief) - ¿Crees que ella viene mañana? (indicative in a question is common because the speaker is asking about a fact as the other person sees it; subjunctive is possible in some contexts, but start with indicative as default after “creer que” in affirmative.) 2) No creo que él sea honesto. (subjunctive) Negated “creer” flips it. You’re not affirming honesty as a fact; you’re rejecting it. 3) Es obvio que tienes razón. (indicative) “Es obvio/evidente/cierto” presents the embedded clause as factual. So indicative. 4) Te llamo cuando llegue. (subjunctive) Here “cuando” introduces a future event that hasn’t happened yet. You’re not describing a habitual present (“I call you when I arrive [as a routine]”), but a future point. Compare: - Te llamo cuando llego. = Every time I arrive, I call you (habitual). - Te llamaré cuando llegue. = I’ll call you when I arrive (future). - Te llamo cuando llegue. = present used with future meaning; still subjunctive because the arriving is in the future. So the “rule” that helps: after certain conjunctions (cuando, en cuanto, hasta que…) if the action is pending/future, use subjunctive; if it’s habitual or past, use indicative.
But why is a question with “creer” usually indicative? If I’m asking, I literally don’t know if it’s true. That feels like uncertainty, so shouldn’t it be subjunctive?

You’re thinking “uncertainty = subjunctive,” which is close but not quite right. Subjunctive is less about whether the world is uncertain and more about whether the speaker is *asserting* the embedded proposition. In a question like: - ¿Crees que ella viene mañana? You’re asking the listener to assert (or not) the proposition “ella viene mañana.” Grammatically, Spanish often treats that as still in the “asserted-as-factual” box, but now the subject of the assertion is the listener, not you. Subjunctive tends to show up in questions when the question itself contains doubt/denial/negative bias, or when the verb inherently triggers it: - ¿Dudas que ella venga mañana? (dudar triggers subjunctive even in questions) - ¿No crees que ella venga mañana? can happen, but it’s more marked and often implies you expect “yes, she is coming” or you’re challenging the listener’s belief. Many speakers prefer indicative even there: ¿No crees que ella viene mañana? So: “creer que” affirmative → indicative; “no creer que” → subjunctive; questions often follow the affirmative pattern unless the question is structured to express doubt/negation in a strong way.
Okay, but then I see stuff like “¿Crees que sea posible…?” or “¿Crees que esté bien…?” and now I’m lost again.

Those examples change the embedded content. The subjunctive there isn’t coming from “crees,” it’s coming from what follows. Compare: - ¿Crees que es posible? (indicative) You’re asking if something is possible as a statement of fact. - ¿Crees que sea posible…? (subjunctive) This can appear when “ser posible” behaves like an evaluation/hypothesis rather than a factual claim. It’s more common in some regions/registers and with “que” clauses that feel like a proposal, conjecture, or “is it even possible that…?” And: - ¿Crees que está bien? vs ¿Crees que esté bien? Both exist; the subjunctive version often means “Do you think it would be okay / appropriate?” (a normative judgment), not “Do you think it is (in reality) okay?” So a useful refinement: - If the embedded clause is about a concrete state/event treated as factual: indicative. - If the embedded clause is about appropriateness, desirability, hypothetical framing, or the speaker is softening/hedging: subjunctive may appear. But if you’re benchmarking yourself, don’t start by trying to predict these nuance cases. Get the big triggers solid first.
I hate that “nuance” is basically code for “no rules.” Give me something operational. Like a checklist I can run mentally.

Operational checklist for a “que” clause: Step 1: Is it a “command/request/influence” verb? - querer que, pedir que, mandar que, recomendar que, sugerir que, prohibir que, dejar que If yes → subjunctive. Step 2: Is it an emotion or value judgment about the clause? - alegrarse de que, sentir que, molestar que, temer que, es triste que, es importante que If yes → subjunctive. Step 3: Is it doubt/denial/uncertainty about the clause? - dudar que, negar que, no creer que, no pensar que, no estar seguro de que, es posible que If yes → subjunctive. Step 4: Is it a statement of fact/knowledge/perception? - saber que, es cierto que, es evidente que, ver que, oír que, notar que If yes → indicative. Step 5: Is it an opinion verb without negation? - creer que, pensar que, opinar que, suponer que If yes → indicative (default). Then you handle the other big areas separately: A) Adverbial clauses with future/pending: cuando, en cuanto, hasta que, antes de que, después de que → subjunctive if pending; indicative if habitual/past. B) Relative clauses with unknown/nonexistent: buscar, necesitar, querer + “alguien/cosa que…” → subjunctive if non-specific; indicative if specific/known. This isn’t perfect, but it’s runnable.
Let’s do relatives because those destroy me. “Busco un libro que explica/explique esto bien.” I’ve seen both and I can’t tell which is “right.”

Both can be right; they encode different assumptions. - Busco un libro que explica esto bien. (indicative) You have a particular book (or you believe such a book exists and you’re thinking of it as real/identified). The clause “que explica…” describes a known category you treat as factual: “a book that explains this well” as a real property of a real book. - Busco un libro que explique esto bien. (subjunctive) You’re looking for any book that would explain it well; you’re not asserting its existence or you don’t have a specific one in mind. It’s like “I need a book that can explain this well.” A diagnostic: If you can replace it with “uno que…” meaning “any one that…,” you’re probably in subjunctive: - Busco un libro, cualquiera que explique esto bien. If you mean “the one that…” or you have it in mind: - Busco el libro que explica esto bien. (definite antecedent → indicative almost always)
So indefinite article pushes subjunctive? Like “un” means subjunctive?

Not automatically. “Un” makes it *possible* to be non-specific, which makes subjunctive common, but you can still be specific with “un” if you have one in mind. Examples: - Busco un profesor que vive en Sevilla. (indicative) = I’m looking for a certain professor (maybe I met them), who lives in Seville. - Busco un profesor que viva en Sevilla. (subjunctive) = I’m looking for any professor who lives in Seville (requirement). So it’s about specificity/known existence, not the article itself.
Then explain “No hay nadie que sabe/sepa la respuesta.” I always want indicative because “nobody knows” feels like a fact.

Spanish treats “no hay X que…” as a classic “nonexistent antecedent” trigger → subjunctive. - No hay nadie que sepa la respuesta. (standard) Because “nadie” means there is no such person; the antecedent doesn’t exist in the discourse. Subjunctive marks that the relative clause is not describing a real, identified person. Indicative “No hay nadie que sabe…” is generally nonstandard or would sound like you’re starting a different structure (some speakers might produce it in spontaneous speech, but for grammar targets, use subjunctive). Same pattern: - No tengo nada que decir. (subjunctive/infinitive) - No existe ningún lugar que sea perfecto.
Okay. Now time clauses. “Después de que” makes me furious. Some books say subjunctive, others show indicative. Which is it?

“Después de que” can take either; it depends on whether the action is completed/known vs pending. 1) Completed past event → indicative: - Después de que terminó la clase, me fui. (It ended; then I left.) - Después de que me llamó, salí. (He called; then I left.) 2) Pending/future event → subjunctive: - Te llamaré después de que termine la clase. (The ending is in the future relative to calling.) - Vamos a salir después de que me llame. (The calling hasn’t happened yet.) The same logic applies to: - antes de que → almost always subjunctive (because it refers to an event not yet realized at the reference time) - cuando / en cuanto / tan pronto como / hasta que → subjunctive for future/pending; indicative for habitual/past.
But “hasta que” confuses me. “No me voy hasta que terminas/termines.” I’ve heard both.

Both can be grammatical with different meanings. - No me voy hasta que termines. (subjunctive) “I’m not leaving until you finish” (finishing is pending, not yet completed). - No me voy hasta que terminas. (indicative) This usually describes a habitual pattern or a scheduled/observed fact in a narrative present: “I don’t leave until you finish (as a rule).” It can also sound like you’re describing what happens in a repeated context. In many real conversations, speakers default to subjunctive because “until you finish” is typically pending. If you’re unsure, choose subjunctive for “hasta que” when it’s about a future completion.
Let’s do “aunque.” That one is a nightmare. I see “aunque es” and “aunque sea” and I can’t tell why.

“Aunque” toggles based on whether the information is presented as known/real vs hypothetical/unknown. Known/real concession → indicative: - Aunque está lloviendo, salgo. (It is raining—fact—and I’m going out anyway.) - Aunque es caro, lo compro. (It is expensive—fact.) Hypothetical/unknown concession → subjunctive: - Aunque llueva, salgo. (Even if it rains—maybe it will, maybe it won’t.) - Aunque sea caro, lo compro. (Even if it’s expensive / even if it turns out to be expensive.) A quick test: if you can paraphrase it as “even if,” subjunctive is likely; if it’s “although” with known facts, indicative.
So “aunque sea” is like “even if it is.” But people also use “aunque sea” meaning “at least,” right? That seems unrelated.

Right—“aunque sea” is also an idiomatic chunk meaning “even if it’s only…” → “at least.” - Dame aunque sea un vaso de agua. = Give me at least a glass of water (even if it’s only that). - Quiero verlo aunque sea cinco minutos. = I want to see him, even if only for five minutes. It still uses subjunctive because it’s framing a minimum concession rather than stating a fact.
I’m still not comfortable. Give me a bunch of minimal pairs where the mood changes the meaning. Like 10 or 15.

Here are 15 minimal pairs with the meaning shift: 1) - Sé que viene. (I know he’s coming.) - No sé que venga. (I don’t know that he’s coming / I’m not aware he’s coming.) (Often: No sé si viene.) 2) - Creo que tiene razón. (I think he’s right.) - No creo que tenga razón. (I don’t think he’s right.) 3) - Es cierto que lo hizo. (It’s true he did it.) - No es cierto que lo hiciera. (It’s not true he did it.) 4) - Busco al estudiante que habla ruso. (Specific student.) - Busco un estudiante que hable ruso. (Any student who speaks Russian.) 5) - Hay alguien que puede ayudarte. (There is someone who can help you.) - No hay nadie que pueda ayudarte. (There is no one who can help you.) 6) - Cuando llega, me llama. (Whenever he arrives, he calls me.) - Cuando llegue, que me llame. / Cuando llegue, me llamará. (When he arrives (in the future), he’ll call.) 7) - Aunque está cansado, trabaja. (He is tired, yet he works.) - Aunque esté cansado, trabajará. (Even if he’s tired, he’ll work.) 8) - Tal vez viene. (Maybe he’s coming.) (Indicative more common in some varieties) - Tal vez venga. (Maybe he’s coming.) (Subjunctive—more uncertainty/less commitment) 9) - Me alegra que estás aquí. (Heard in some speech, but prescriptive prefers subjunctive) - Me alegra que estés aquí. (I’m glad you’re here.) 10) - Pienso que es verdad. (I think it’s true.) - No pienso que sea verdad. (I don’t think it’s true.) 11) - Está claro que necesitamos más tiempo. (It’s clear we need more time.) - No está claro que necesitemos más tiempo. (It’s not clear we need more time.) 12) - Te llamaré después de que termino. (Wrong/odd for future) - Te llamaré después de que termine. (Correct: after I finish.) 13) - Lo haré si tengo tiempo. (If I have time—real condition, possible) - Lo haría si tuviera tiempo. (If I had time—contrary-to-fact/hypothetical) 14) - Espero que viene mañana. (Nonstandard; should be subjunctive) - Espero que venga mañana. (I hope he comes tomorrow.) 15) - Puede ser que es tarde. (Often corrected) - Puede ser que sea tarde. (It may be late.) Some of these include prescriptive notes because colloquial speech varies, but as a learner aiming for reliable grammar, the subjunctive options shown are the safe ones.
Wait, #1 looks weird. “No sé que venga” sounds wrong to me. Isn’t it “No sé si viene”?

You’re right to flag it. “No sé que venga” is not the normal way to say it. The normal options are: - No sé si viene. (I don’t know if he’s coming.) - No sé que venga. is generally incorrect in standard Spanish unless it’s part of a different construction (like “No sé que venga nadie” in some dialectal uses), but as a general “I don’t know that…” it’s not the go-to. So use: - Sé que viene. (indicative) - No sé si viene. (indicative after si) And for “I don’t believe that…”: - No creo que venga. (subjunctive) Good catch.
Great. So “si” uses indicative, right? Always?

In indirect questions, “si” (whether/if) takes indicative: - No sé si viene. - Pregúntale si tiene tiempo. - Dime si lo viste. But “si” also appears in conditional sentences (“if”), and then the tense/mood depends on the type of condition: - Si tengo tiempo, voy. (real/possible → present indicative) - Si tenía tiempo, iba. (habitual past/sequence) - Si tuviera tiempo, iría. (hypothetical → imperfect subjunctive) - Si hubiera tenido tiempo, habría ido. (counterfactual past → pluperfect subjunctive) So: “si” in indirect questions → indicative; “si” in conditionals → indicative or subjunctive depending on reality/hypothesis.
I mess up the imperfect subjunctive too. “Si tendría tiempo” keeps popping out of my mouth and then I remember it’s wrong. Why is it wrong?

Because Spanish marks the hypothetical part with subjunctive, and the result with conditional. Correct patterns: - Si tuviera tiempo, iría. (If I had time, I would go.) - Si tuviese tiempo, iría. (same; -ra / -se variants) Incorrect in standard Spanish: - Si tendría tiempo, iría. Why? The conditional “tendría” already expresses the result/conditionality; Spanish doesn’t put conditional in the “si” clause in standard usage. The “si” clause uses imperfect subjunctive for present/future hypothetical, and pluperfect subjunctive for past counterfactual.
Okay, but then I hear “Si tendría…” from some speakers. Is that just wrong or regional?

It’s widely considered nonstandard, but it does appear in some regions and in informal speech. For benchmarking/standard grammar, treat it as incorrect and stick to: - Si tuviera/tuviese…, (yo) tendría/haría/iría…
Back to present subjunctive. Can you give me a clean conjugation reminder for irregulars? Because half my errors are just forms.

High-frequency present subjunctive irregulars and patterns: 1) Yo-forms that are irregular in present indicative carry over their stem to all subjunctive forms (except nosotros/vosotros keep the same stem but still follow subjunctive endings): - tener → tenga, tengas, tenga, tengamos, tengáis, tengan - venir → venga… - decir → diga… - hacer → haga… - poner → ponga… - salir → salga… - traer → traiga… - oír → oiga… - caer → caiga… - conocer → conozca… - conducir → conduzca… 2) Completely irregular: - ser → sea, seas, sea, seamos, seáis, sean - ir → vaya, vayas, vaya, vayamos, vayáis, vayan - estar → esté, estés, esté, estemos, estéis, estén - haber → haya, hayas, haya, hayamos, hayáis, hayan - saber → sepa, sepas, sepa, sepamos, sepáis, sepan - dar → dé, des, dé, demos, deis, den 3) Spelling changes for -car/-gar/-zar: - buscar → busque - pagar → pague - empezar → empiece (also stem change) 4) Stem changes: - e→ie: pensar → piense; querer → quiera; cerrar → cierre - o→ue: poder → pueda; dormir → duerma - e→i: pedir → pida; servir → sirva - -ir verbs also change in nosotros/vosotros: dormir → durmamos/durmáis; pedir → pidamos/pidáis If you want, list your top 10 verbs and I’ll write them in the subjunctive so you can drill.
Before drilling, I want clarity on triggers that look similar but flip mood. Like “es seguro que” vs “no es seguro que.” That one I can’t internalize.

That pair is a textbook flip: - Es seguro que + indicative You’re asserting certainty: “It’s certain that…” - No es seguro que + subjunctive You’re saying it’s not certain; uncertainty triggers subjunctive. Examples: - Es seguro que viene hoy. (It’s certain he’s coming today.) - No es seguro que venga hoy. (It’s not certain he’ll come today.) Same pattern: - Es probable que venga. (probable → subjunctive because it’s not asserted fact) - Es cierto que viene. (certain/true → indicative) - No es cierto que venga / que haya venido. (denial → subjunctive)
But “probable” feels like “almost sure,” so why not indicative?

Because “probable” is still an evaluation of likelihood, not a declaration of fact. Spanish generally reserves indicative for when the speaker frames it as true/real, not just likely. That said, you’ll sometimes see indicative with “probablemente” as an adverb: - Probablemente viene mañana. (indicative) - Probablemente venga mañana. (subjunctive) Both can appear; the indicative version can sound more like the speaker is leaning toward it as a working assumption. But with the impersonal expression “Es probable que…”, subjunctive is the standard.
Okay. Now “esperar.” Why is it always subjunctive? If I say “Espero que vienes,” that’s wrong, but I kind of mean “I expect you’re coming.”

Spanish distinguishes: - esperar = to hope → subjunctive - esperar = to expect (in the sense of “anticipate”) can still take subjunctive in many cases, but Spanish often uses different verbs to avoid ambiguity: “suponer,” “imaginar,” “creer,” “pensar,” “tener la esperanza de que…” Standard: - Espero que vengas mañana. (I hope you come tomorrow.) - Espero que estés bien. (I hope you’re well.) If you mean “I expect/assume you’re coming” (belief about a fact), use indicative with a belief verb: - Supongo que vienes mañana. - Imagino que vienes mañana. - Creo que vienes mañana. “Espero que vienes” is typically corrected because it mixes the “hope” frame with an indicative assertion.
That helps. What about “temer que”? I see both moods depending on whether it’s “I fear that” or “I’m afraid that,” but those are the same in English.

“Temer que” generally triggers subjunctive because it’s fear/emotion + uncertainty: - Temo que llegue tarde. (I’m afraid he’ll arrive late.) But Spanish also has “me temo que” which often behaves like “I suspect / I’m afraid (I think) that…” and can go indicative, especially when the speaker is presenting it as a conclusion: - Me temo que tienes razón. (I’m afraid you’re right.) (indicative—treating it as true) - Me temo que sea verdad. (subjunctive—more hypothetical/less committed) So: - temer que (fear of something happening) → subjunctive is the safe default. - me temo que + indicative often signals “unfortunately, I think it’s true.”
So “me temo que” is like a belief verb sometimes. Annoying, but at least it’s a pattern.
Exactly: it can slide toward “creo que (lamentablemente).”
Can we talk about “como si”? I know it uses imperfect subjunctive, but I don’t get why it’s always past-looking even when it’s present.

“Como si” introduces an unreal comparison: “as if.” Spanish marks unreality with past subjunctive forms. Present unreal comparison → imperfect subjunctive: - Habla como si supiera todo. (He talks as if he knew everything.) (He doesn’t.) - Me mira como si no me conociera. (as if he didn’t know me) Past unreal comparison → pluperfect subjunctive: - Habló como si hubiera visto un fantasma. (as if he had seen a ghost) It’s not about past time; it’s about distance from reality. Spanish uses the imperfect/pluperfect subjunctive to signal that.
Okay. Now, “ojalá.” I know it’s subjunctive, but I never know which tense. “Ojalá venga” vs “ojalá viniera” vs “ojalá hubiera venido.”

Think of “ojalá” as “I hope / if only,” and pick the tense based on time + realism: 1) Present/future hope (possible) → present subjunctive: - Ojalá venga mañana. (I hope he comes tomorrow.) - Ojalá no llueva. (I hope it doesn’t rain.) 2) Present wish that is contrary to reality / more remote → imperfect subjunctive: - Ojalá tuviera más tiempo. (If only I had more time.) - Ojalá estuvieras aquí. (If only you were here.) 3) Past regret (didn’t happen) → pluperfect subjunctive: - Ojalá hubiera venido. (If only he had come.) - Ojalá no lo hubiera dicho. (If only I hadn’t said it.)
Great. Now I need you to correct a paragraph I wrote, but also explain every subjunctive decision. Don’t just rewrite it.
Paste it and I’ll go sentence by sentence: (1) corrected version, (2) mood explanation, (3) any alternative phrasing if applicable.

“Creo que sea importante que la gente aprende español bien. Es obvio que muchos no tienen tiempo, pero quiero que estudian un poco cada día. Cuando tienen un examen, es posible que están nerviosos. Les digo que no se preocupan y que practican. También busco un método que es simple y que funciona para todos. Aunque es difícil, espero que pueden mejorar pronto.”

1) Corrected paragraph (keeping your meaning): “Creo que es importante que la gente aprenda español bien. Es obvio que muchos no tienen tiempo, pero quiero que estudien un poco cada día. Cuando tienen un examen, es posible que estén nerviosos. Les digo que no se preocupen y que practiquen. También busco un método que sea simple y que funcione para todos. Aunque es difícil, espero que puedan mejorar pronto.” 2) Explanations, clause by clause: - “Creo que es importante…” → indicative “es” Because “creo que” (affirmative belief) takes indicative. You’re asserting your opinion as a statement. - “…que la gente aprenda…” → subjunctive “aprenda” Because “es importante que…” is an impersonal value judgment; it triggers subjunctive in the subordinate clause. - “Es obvio que muchos no tienen tiempo” → indicative “tienen” “Es obvio que” asserts a fact as evident. - “quiero que estudien…” → subjunctive “estudien” Volition/influence: querer que + subjunctive. - “Cuando tienen un examen…” → indicative “tienen” This is habitual/general time: “when they have an exam (in general).” Not a future pending event tied to one specific future exam. - “es posible que estén nerviosos” → subjunctive “estén” Possibility/uncertainty: es posible que + subjunctive. - “Les digo que no se preocupen y que practiquen” → subjunctive in both Because “decir” here functions like giving an instruction (“I tell them not to worry / to practice”). In that meaning (“tell someone to do something”), Spanish uses subjunctive. If “decir” is just reporting information, it would be indicative: - Les digo que están nerviosos. (I tell them that they are nervous.) (indicative) - “busco un método que sea simple y que funcione…” → subjunctive “sea/funcione” Non-specific antecedent: you’re searching for a method that meets requirements; you’re not pointing to a known method. If you meant a specific method you already know exists (or you have it in mind), you could use indicative: - Busco el método que es simple y funciona… - “Aunque es difícil…” → indicative “es” Here you’re presenting it as a known fact: it is difficult. If you meant “even if it’s difficult,” hypothetical, you could use subjunctive: - Aunque sea difícil, … - “espero que puedan mejorar…” → subjunctive “puedan” Hope/wish: esperar que + subjunctive.
The “les digo que” part is interesting. So “decir que” can be indicative or subjunctive depending on whether it’s reporting or commanding?

Exactly. Two different constructions that look similar: A) Reporting information (asserting content) → indicative: - Le digo que tengo hambre. (I tell him that I’m hungry.) - Les dije que mañana hay clase. (I told them that there’s class tomorrow.) B) Giving an instruction/request (influence) → subjunctive: - Le digo que venga. (I tell him to come.) - Les dije que estudiaran. (I told them to study.) A quick trick: if you can replace it with an imperative in English (“I tell them: ‘Study!’”), Spanish will likely want subjunctive.
What about “decirle a alguien que…” with a noun after? Like “Le digo que la tarea es difícil.” That’s reporting, right?

Yes: - Le digo que la tarea es difícil. (I tell him the homework is difficult.) → indicative “es” But: - Le digo que haga la tarea. (I tell him to do the homework.) → subjunctive “haga” Same verb, different function.
Give me a drill: 25 sentences where I choose mood, and then you give the answers with explanations. Make them hard, not the obvious “quiero que.”

25 choose-the-mood items. Pick indicative or subjunctive (and correct tense) for the verb in parentheses. 1) Es raro que Marta no (venir) _____ hoy. 2) Me parece que Juan (tener) _____ razón. 3) No me parece que Juan (tener) _____ razón. 4) Buscamos a alguien que (poder) _____ traducir este texto. 5) Conozco a alguien que (poder) _____ traducir este texto. 6) Te llamaré en cuanto (llegar) _____ a casa. 7) Siempre te llamo en cuanto (llegar) _____ a casa. 8) Dudo que ellos (saber) _____ la verdad. 9) No dudo que ellos (saber) _____ la verdad. 10) Es posible que ya (salir) _____. 11) Es evidente que ya (salir) _____. 12) No es evidente que ya (salir) _____. 13) Aunque (ser) _____ tarde, seguimos trabajando. (It is late—fact) 14) Aunque (ser) _____ tarde, seguiremos trabajando. (Even if it’s late) 15) No hay ninguna razón para que tú (hacer) _____ eso. 16) Hay una razón por la que tú (hacer) _____ eso. 17) Insisten en que nosotros (ir) _____ con ellos. 18) Pienso que nosotros (ir) _____ con ellos. 19) ¿Crees que él (estar) _____ enfermo? 20) ¿No crees que él (estar) _____ enfermo? (You suspect he is) 21) Me alegra que me (decir) _____ la verdad. 22) Me molesta que siempre me (interrumpir) _____. 23) Lo haré antes de que tú me lo (pedir) _____. 24) Tal vez (tener) _____ tiempo mañana. 25) No es que yo (no querer) _____ ayudarte; es que no puedo. Reply with your choices (just the conjugated verbs). Then I’ll give the key with explanations.

1 venga, 2 tiene, 3 tenga, 4 pueda, 5 puede, 6 llegue, 7 llego, 8 sepan, 9 saben, 10 haya salido, 11 ha salido, 12 haya salido, 13 es, 14 sea, 15 hagas, 16 haces, 17 vayamos, 18 vamos, 19 está, 20 está, 21 dijeras, 22 interrumpes, 23 pidas, 24 tengo, 25 no quiera.

Mostly excellent. Corrections and explanations: 1) venga ✅ Subjunctive after “es raro que” (evaluation). 2) tiene ✅ “me parece que” affirmative opinion → indicative. 3) tenga ✅ negated opinion → subjunctive. 4) pueda ✅ non-specific “alguien” sought → subjunctive. 5) puede ✅ known person → indicative. 6) llegue ✅ “en cuanto” + future event → subjunctive. 7) llego ✅ habitual → indicative. 8) sepan ✅ “dudo que” → subjunctive. 9) saben ✅ “no dudo que” expresses certainty → indicative. 10) haya salido ✅ possibility about a completed action → present perfect subjunctive. 11) ha salido ✅ “es evidente que” factual → indicative perfect. 12) haya salido ✅ “no es evidente que” uncertainty → subjunctive. 13) es ✅ known fact concession → indicative. 14) sea ✅ hypothetical concession → subjunctive. 15) hagas ✅ “para que” purpose → subjunctive. 16) haces ✅ “la razón por la que…” is factual explanation; indicative is standard. 17) vayamos ✅ “insistir en que” influence → subjunctive. 18) vamos ✅ “pienso que” affirmative → indicative. 19) está ✅ default after “¿Crees que…?” asking about a state (treated as factual claim by listener). 20) está ✅ In the “you suspect he is” reading, indicative is very natural: “¿No crees que está enfermo?” Subjunctive can occur but is more marked; your choice is fine. 21) me dijeras ❌ should be “hayas dicho” (or “me dijiste” in a different structure) Because “Me alegra que…” refers to a prior completed action “that you told me the truth.” With current relevance, Spanish uses present perfect subjunctive: - Me alegra que me hayas dicho la verdad. “Dijeras” (imperfect subjunctive) would fit if the main verb were past or in a conditional frame: - Me alegró que me dijeras la verdad. - Me alegraría que me dijeras la verdad. 22) interrumpes ❌ should be “interrumpas” “Me molesta que…” emotion/annoyance → subjunctive even if it’s habitual: - Me molesta que siempre me interrumpas. Indicative here is a common learner error because it feels like a fact, but the grammar treats it as the cause of an emotional reaction. 23) pidas ✅ “antes de que” → subjunctive. 24) tengo ✅ Both “tal vez tenga” and “tal vez tengo” exist depending on dialect/commitment. Your indicative is acceptable; subjunctive would show more uncertainty. 25) no quiera ✅ “No es que…” typically introduces a negated/defensive explanation → subjunctive. If you want a second set, I can make one focused on tense choice (present vs perfect vs imperfect subjunctive), since that’s where #21 came from.
Yes, tense choice is exactly where I fall apart. I never know when to use present perfect subjunctive vs imperfect subjunctive in these “emotion” phrases.

Use this anchor: the tense in the subjunctive clause is mostly relative to the tense of the main verb and to whether the subordinate action is prior/completed vs simultaneous/future. Common patterns: A) Main verb in present (me alegra / me molesta / es bueno)… 1) Subordinate simultaneous or future → present subjunctive: - Me alegra que estés aquí. (simultaneous) - Espero que vengas mañana. (future) 2) Subordinate already completed (recent/past with relevance) → present perfect subjunctive: - Me alegra que hayas venido. (you already came) - Me molesta que no me hayas llamado. (you didn’t call) B) Main verb in past (me alegró / me molestó / era bueno)… 1) Subordinate simultaneous or future relative to that past moment → imperfect subjunctive: - Me alegró que estuvieras allí. (at that time) - Quería que vinieras. (future-in-the-past) 2) Subordinate completed before that past moment → pluperfect subjunctive: - Me alegró que hubieras venido. (you had come before that point) - Lamenté que no me hubieras llamado. So: present main verb → use present or present perfect subjunctive; past main verb → use imperfect or pluperfect subjunctive.
Then why is “Me alegra que me dijeras la verdad” wrong? It’s present main verb, but imperfect subjunctive sounds okay to my ear.

Because with “me alegra” (present), “dijeras” pushes the telling into a past frame that doesn’t match the usual sequence. Standard Spanish prefers: - Me alegra que me hayas dicho la verdad. (completed before now) “Me alegra que me dijeras…” is sometimes heard in some varieties as a stylistic or colloquial sequence, but for standard sequencing, present main clause pairs with present perfect subjunctive for a completed prior event. If you switch the main verb to past, “dijeras” becomes correct: - Me alegró que me dijeras la verdad. (At that time, I was glad you told me.)
Okay. I need more examples with the same main verb but different subordinate tenses so I can see contrasts.

Using “Me alegra que…”: 1) Present subjunctive (simultaneous): - Me alegra que estés aquí. (You are here now.) 2) Present perfect subjunctive (prior completed): - Me alegra que hayas venido. (You already came.) 3) Imperfect subjunctive would normally require a past/conditional main verb: - Me alegraba que estuvieras aquí. (In the past, it made me glad you were there.) - Me alegraría que estuvieras aquí. (I would be glad if you were here.) 4) Pluperfect subjunctive with a past main verb: - Me alegró que hubieras venido. (You had come before that past point; less common but possible in narratives.) Another with “Espero que…”: - Espero que llegues a tiempo. (future) - Espero que hayas llegado bien. (by now, you have arrived safely)
That “Espero que hayas llegado bien” is interesting. It’s like checking on someone after the fact. So present perfect subjunctive can be like “I hope you’ve arrived.”
Exactly: the event is completed (arrival), but you’re expressing hope about its outcome, not asserting it as fact.
Can we do a set of 20 sentences specifically to choose between present subjunctive vs present perfect subjunctive vs imperfect subjunctive? And please include some with “quizás/tal vez” too.

Choose the best form for the verb in parentheses. 1) Me alegra que (venir) _____. 2) Me alegra que (estar) _____ aquí ahora. 3) Espero que (terminar) _____ pronto. 4) Espero que ya (terminar) _____. 5) Es una lástima que no me (llamar) _____. 6) Es una lástima que no me (llamar) _____ ayer cuando pudiste. 7) Me sorprendió que (decir) _____ eso. 8) Me sorprende que (decir) _____ eso. 9) Quería que tú (poder) _____ venir. 10) Quiero que tú (poder) _____ venir. 11) Dudo que (ver) _____ la película todavía. 12) Dudo que (ver) _____ la película mañana. 13) Es posible que (salir) _____ ya. 14) Es posible que (salir) _____ mañana temprano. 15) Quizás (tener) _____ razón, pero no estoy seguro. 16) Quizás (haber) _____ un error en el cálculo. 17) No creía que (ser) _____ tan difícil. 18) No creo que (ser) _____ tan difícil. 19) Me molestaba que siempre me (interrumpir) _____. 20) Me molesta que siempre me (interrumpir) _____.

1 hayas venido, 2 estés, 3 termines, 4 hayas terminado, 5 hayas llamado, 6 llamaras, 7 dijeras, 8 hayas dicho, 9 pudieras, 10 puedas, 11 hayas visto, 12 veas, 13 haya salido, 14 salga, 15 tienes, 16 haya habido, 17 fuera, 18 sea, 19 interrumpieras, 20 interrumpas.

Answer key with quick reasoning: 1) hayas venido ✅ completed before now. 2) estés ✅ simultaneous. 3) termines ✅ future relative to now. 4) hayas terminado ✅ already completed by now. 5) hayas llamado ✅ not called up to now (completed non-event). 6) llamaras ✅ “ayer” anchors it in past; present main clause + imperfect subjunctive can appear when the time is clearly past and closed; many speakers still prefer present perfect if it’s “recent,” but with “ayer” imperfect is very natural. 7) dijeras ✅ main verb past “me sorprendió” → imperfect subjunctive. 8) hayas dicho ✅ present main verb + completed utterance → present perfect subjunctive. 9) pudieras ✅ past main “quería” → imperfect subjunctive. 10) puedas ✅ present main “quiero” → present subjunctive. 11) hayas visto ✅ “todavía” suggests up-to-now completion. 12) veas ✅ future (tomorrow) → present subjunctive. 13) haya salido ✅ “ya” + possibility about completed action. 14) salga ✅ future (tomorrow) → present subjunctive. 15) tienes ✅ acceptable; “quizás” can take indicative when speaker leans toward it as plausible. Subjunctive “tengas” would sound more doubtful. Your choice matches “pero no estoy seguro” fine. 16) haya habido ✅ possibility of a past/completed error → present perfect subjunctive. 17) fuera ✅ past belief “no creía” → imperfect subjunctive. 18) sea ✅ present “no creo” → present subjunctive. 19) interrumpieras ✅ past ongoing annoyance “me molestaba” → imperfect subjunctive. 20) interrumpas ✅ present annoyance → present subjunctive. You’re choosing tense well. The only one that’s stylistically variable is #6; your “llamaras” is good with “ayer.”
Now I want you to explain the weird cases with “es posible que” + indicative. I’ve seen “Es posible que es…” in some comments online. Is that just wrong?

In standard Spanish, “es posible que” takes subjunctive: - Es posible que sea verdad. - Es posible que estén en casa. “Es posible que es…” is generally treated as incorrect in prescriptive grammar. You may see it in informal writing or from speakers influenced by other patterns, but for reliable usage, stick with subjunctive after “es posible que.” What can take indicative is “es posible” used without “que,” or other rephrases: - Es posible. (It’s possible.) - Es posible esto: que… (then the “que” clause may vary, but that’s a different structure)
Another one: “Puede que.” Same deal?
Yes: - Puede que venga. - Puede que haya llegado. Subjunctive is standard.
I also struggle with “lo que” and “el hecho de que.” Like “El hecho de que viene/venga…” Which mood?

“El hecho de que” often triggers subjunctive when it’s used to evaluate or point out something as relevant/cause for reaction, especially with a main clause expressing emotion/judgment: - Me molesta el hecho de que venga tarde. (subjunctive) - Es extraño el hecho de que no venga. (subjunctive) But it can take indicative when “el hecho de que” is used to assert a fact, more in formal contexts: - El hecho de que vino tarde es indiscutible. (indicative, factual assertion) So the mood depends on whether the speaker is treating it as an established fact vs framing it as a subject of evaluation/attitude. In everyday usage, “el hecho de que + subjunctive” is very common. As for “lo que,” it’s a relative pronoun; mood depends on the verb and structure, not “lo que” itself: - No entiendo lo que dices. (indicative: you are saying it) - No entiendo lo que quieras decir. (subjunctive: “what you might mean / are trying to say”)
That last pair is exactly the kind of thing that makes me feel like I’ll never “get it.” Because both are plausible.

They are, and the difference is subtle but real: - No entiendo lo que dices. = I don’t understand what you’re saying (content is there; I can’t parse it). - No entiendo lo que quieras decir. = I don’t understand what you mean / what you’re trying to say (your intended meaning is not clear; it’s like a potential interpretation). A lot of subjunctive usage lives in that zone: not denying reality, but signaling “not pinned down as a concrete fact.”
Give me more of those “not pinned down” examples. Not the obvious trigger words.

Here are several where subjunctive appears without the classic “quiero que” style trigger, often to express indeterminacy, limitation, or “whatever/wherever”: 1) Sea lo que sea, no voy. (Whatever it is, I’m not going.) 2) Hagas lo que hagas, te criticarán. (Whatever you do…) 3) Diga lo que diga, no le creen. (No matter what he says…) 4) Donde sea que estés, llámame. (Wherever you are…) 5) Como sea, hay que decidir. (Either way / however it is, we must decide.) 6) No importa lo que digas. (It doesn’t matter what you say.) (Often subjunctive in some variants: “digas” is already subjunctive form; structure is fixed.) 7) Elige el que quieras. (Choose whichever you want.) 8) Hazlo cuando puedas. (Do it whenever you can.) (subjunctive because “whenever you are able,” not a specific time) 9) No veo a nadie que quiera ayudar. (No one who wants to help—nonexistence) 10) Cualquier cosa que digas será usada… (Whatever you say will be used…) These are “free relative” or “indefinite relative” constructions; subjunctive marks the open-endedness.

“Cuando puedas” is subjunctive but it’s basically future/hypothetical. Okay. But why is “No importa lo que digas” subjunctive? That seems like it’s about what you will say, but you might be saying something right now.

In “No importa lo que digas,” the structure “lo que + subjunctive” functions like “whatever you say,” meaning the content is irrelevant and not being treated as a specific asserted proposition. It’s a generalizing device, not a time reference. If you want indicative, you’d need a different meaning: - No importa lo que dices. would sound like “It doesn’t matter what you are saying (right now, specifically),” but it’s much less idiomatic; Spanish prefers the subjunctive generalization.
I think my real problem is I keep trying to map it to English. English doesn’t have this.

Right approach is to stop translating and instead classify the subordinate clause as either: - asserted/identified (indicative), or - non-asserted/non-identified/desired/hypothetical (subjunctive). English uses modal verbs, word choice, and intonation to do some of this; Spanish encodes it grammatically.
Can you give me a one-page summary I can memorize? Like bullet points, but not vague.

Indicative vs Subjunctive (memorize-friendly): Use INDICATIVE when the subordinate clause is presented as a fact/claim/known information: - Knowledge/perception: saber que, ver que, oír que, darse cuenta de que - Certainty: es cierto/evidente/obvio/verdad que - Affirmative opinion/belief: creer que, pensar que, suponer que (affirmative) - Habitual time clauses: cuando + present (habitual), siempre que + habitual, etc. - Specific/known relative clauses: el/la/los/las + que…, alguien que (known person) Use SUBJUNCTIVE when the subordinate clause is not asserted as fact: 1) Will/influence (someone wants/asks/orders): - querer que, pedir que, recomendar que, insistir en que, prohibir que, permitir que 2) Emotion/reaction/judgment: - alegrarse de que, molestar que, temer que - es bueno/importante/raro/triste/una lástima que 3) Doubt/denial/uncertainty: - dudar que, negar que, no creer que, no pensar que - no es cierto/evidente/seguro que - es posible/probable que, puede que 4) Time/condition referring to the future/pending: - cuando, en cuanto, hasta que, antes de que, después de que (future) → subjunctive - if habitual/past → indicative 5) Relative clauses with non-specific/unknown/nonexistent antecedent: - busco/necesito/quiero algo/alguien que + subjunctive - no hay nadie/nada que + subjunctive 6) Concession/hypothesis: - aunque + subjunctive = even if / whether or not - aunque + indicative = although (known fact) Tense sequencing quick guide: - Main verb present → subordinate: present subjunctive (simultaneous/future) or present perfect subjunctive (completed). - Main verb past/conditional → subordinate: imperfect subjunctive (simultaneous/future-in-past) or pluperfect subjunctive (completed before that past point).
That’s actually helpful. I’m still mad that I have to think this much, but whatever. One more: “para que” always subjunctive, yes?

Yes: “para que” + subjunctive (purpose with different subject): - Lo hago para que estés contento. - Estudia para que apruebes. If the subject is the same, Spanish usually uses “para” + infinitive: - Estudio para aprobar. - Lo hago para estar contento.
And “sin que” and “a menos que” are also always subjunctive?
