Yes, both are standardly subjunctive: - sin que + subjunctive: Se fue sin que nadie lo viera. Lo hizo sin que yo me diera cuenta. - a menos que + subjunctive: Iré a menos que llueva. No lo haré a menos que me lo pidas.
What about “porque”? Sometimes I see subjunctive after “porque” and it makes me want to throw my book.

“Porque” is usually indicative, but subjunctive appears when the “because” clause is not presented as an accepted cause—often when it’s negated or questioned, or when it’s a supposed/unknown reason. Indicative (real cause): - Lo hice porque tenía tiempo. (I did it because I had time.) Subjunctive (not the real cause / disputed cause / unknown cause): - No lo hice porque tuviera tiempo. (I didn’t do it because I had time [implies: I did it for another reason].) - ¿Lo hiciste porque tuvieras miedo? (Were you doing it because you were afraid?—speaker treats cause as hypothesis) So: subjunctive after “porque” often signals “not because (that reason)” or “because (maybe).”
That “No lo hice porque tuviera tiempo” is super counterintuitive. It sounds like “I didn’t do it because I had time,” which in English means the opposite.

Exactly—Spanish uses that construction to negate the *causal link*, not necessarily the action. It often implies the action did happen, but not for that reason: - No lo hice porque tuviera tiempo, sino porque era necesario. = I didn’t do it because I had time, but because it was necessary. If you mean you didn’t do it, and the reason is you didn’t have time, you’d say: - No lo hice porque no tenía tiempo. (indicative; real cause is lack of time) So: - porque + subjunctive under negation = “not because (that alleged reason)” - porque + indicative = actual cause.
Okay, that one is a real rule. I can work with that.
Good. If you want to solidify it, write 5 sentences with “porque” where the subjunctive is required, and 5 where it’s indicative, and I’ll check them.
Not now. I’m exhausted. But I’ll ask one last thing: Is there any “cheat” where I can just use indicative and still be understood? Because I feel like I’ll never speak if I keep pausing.

You’ll usually be understood with indicative, but you’ll sound off, and sometimes you’ll change meaning (especially with commands, purpose clauses, and conditionals). If you need a practical “don’t freeze” strategy: - Prioritize getting subjunctive right in: (1) commands/influence (quiero que, pido que), (2) purpose (para que), (3) future time clauses (cuando llegue), (4) hypothetical conditionals (si tuviera…). Those are high-impact; indicative there can confuse. In lower-impact areas (some “tal vez,” some nuance after “creer” in questions), you can default to indicative and still be understood, though it won’t be perfect.
That’s fair. I’ll drill those. I’m still irritated, but at least I’m less lost than before.

Why does Japanese need three writing systems? Wouldn't one alphabet be more efficient?

“Need” is a bit misleading—Japanese ended up with three main scripts because they solve different problems that arise from the language’s structure and historical constraints. If you tried to force Japanese into a single alphabetic system, you’d quickly run into ambiguity (too many homophones), morphological issues (how to show word boundaries and inflection), and readability problems (long strings of syllables with few cues). The three systems are: - Kanji: logographic characters that encode meaning and help disambiguate homophones. - Hiragana: a phonetic syllabary used for native grammatical endings, function words, and many native words. - Katakana: a phonetic syllabary used for foreign loanwords, emphasis, scientific names, and stylistic signaling. A single alphabet could represent sounds, but Japanese has relatively few syllables and a lot of words that sound the same. Kanji provides semantic anchors; kana provides phonetic and grammatical scaffolding.
But couldn’t Japanese just use kana only, like hiragana everywhere? That seems simple.

It can, and historically there have been kana-only texts (children’s materials, some signage, certain literature). But “simple” comes with trade-offs. The core issue is the phonotactics of Japanese: most syllables are open (CV) with limited consonant clusters, and the inventory is small. That means many unrelated words collapse into identical kana strings. For example, こうしょう (kōshō) could correspond to multiple words: 交渉 (negotiation), 校章 (school emblem), 高尚 (high-minded), 工廠 (arsenal/factory), and more. In kana-only writing, you’d need more context to disambiguate, and reading becomes slower because you have to parse longer sequences and resolve ambiguity mentally. Kanji chunks text into meaning-bearing units. Hiragana then marks inflection and grammatical particles. The resulting mixture gives visual segmentation: you can often see where content words are and where grammar is, even before fully parsing.
So kanji is mostly about disambiguation? Is that the main reason?

Disambiguation is a major functional reason, but not the only one. 1) Disambiguation (homophones): Japanese has many homophones due to limited syllables and historical sound changes. Kanji keep them distinct. 2) Efficient visual parsing: Kanji are visually dense; a single character can represent a morpheme. That reduces character count and provides sharp boundaries. Compare: - けいざいせいちょう (keizai seichō) vs 経済成長 The kanji version is shorter and visually structured. 3) Morphological clarity: Japanese is agglutinative. Verbs and adjectives take endings that express tense, politeness, negation, etc. Hiragana is well suited to write these inflections consistently: - 食べる, 食べない, 食べました Kanji for the stem (食) plus hiragana endings. 4) Lexical signaling: Different scripts can signal word origin or style. Katakana often marks loanwords, onomatopoeia, technical terms, or emphasis, which helps the reader predict pronunciation and semantic domain. So: kanji helps with meaning and segmentation, kana helps with grammar and sound, and katakana adds a layer of lexical labeling.
If kanji helps with meaning, why not write everything in kanji then?

Two main reasons: practicality and linguistic fit. Practicality: You’d need an enormous character inventory to represent all morphemes and function words distinctly. Japanese grammar uses many particles (は, が, を, に, で, へ, と, も, から, まで) and inflectional endings (ない, たい, ます, た, て, れる, られる). Writing those in kanji would either require inventing or standardizing many characters that aren’t historically established for those grammatical elements, or repurposing existing ones in ways that could confuse readers. Linguistic fit: Function words and inflections are high-frequency and often phonologically reduced; kana is ideal for writing them consistently. Also, some words have conventional kanji spellings that are rare or obscure; writing them in kana can actually be clearer. There is also an aesthetic/legibility aspect: pure-kanji text would be visually dense and potentially hard to scan for grammar, because you’d lose the immediate cue that hiragana provides for particles and endings.
Okay, then why have two kana systems? Why not just hiragana for everything phonetic?

Katakana exists partly due to its origin and partly because it’s useful as a distinct typographic channel. Historically, both hiragana and katakana emerged as shorthand ways to annotate Chinese texts. Over time, hiragana became associated with more flowing cursive forms and general native writing, while katakana developed from angular fragments of kanji used as reading aids. Functionally today, having two phonetic scripts allows Japanese to encode extra information without changing pronunciation: - Loanwords: コンピュータ, インターネット, アルゴリズム. - Foreign names and places (in general writing): ロバート, マリア. - Scientific species names, technical jargon, minerals, etc.: ナトリウム (sodium), イヌ科 (canid family uses katakana for animal names often). - Emphasis akin to italics: これはマジで大事. - Onomatopoeia: ドキドキ, ワクワク, ザーザー. If everything phonetic were in hiragana, you’d lose these visual cues. Katakana acts like a built-in typographic mode.
You mentioned Japanese has lots of homophones. Is that because of the writing system or independent?

Independent. The homophones arise from phonology and historical sound change, not from writing per se. Japanese has: - A relatively small set of morae (units like か, き, く… plus ん, small っ, long vowels). - Many borrowed words from Chinese over centuries. When Chinese morphemes were borrowed, they were adapted to Japanese phonology, collapsing distinctions that existed in Chinese. Many different Chinese syllables ended up mapping to the same Japanese syllable sequences. Also, sound changes within Japanese reduced distinctions over time. So you get lots of words like しょうこう, こうしょう, せいこう, etc. Kanji helps keep these apart in writing.
Can you quantify the “small set of morae”? How small is it?

Roughly speaking, modern standard Japanese has on the order of ~100 basic morae if you count the gojūon grid (a i u e o; ka ki ku ke ko; etc.) plus diacritics (ga, za, da, ba, pa), plus contracted sounds (きゃ, きゅ, きょ; etc.), plus the moraic nasal ん, the sokuon small っ, and long vowels (which are combinations like おう / う or a long vowel mark in katakana). The exact number depends on how you count: - Basic CV morae: about 46 “core” kana (not counting small versions). - Add voiced/semi-voiced: +25 or so. - Add yōon combinations (kya, kyu, kyo…): dozens more. Still, compared to languages with many consonant clusters and larger syllable inventories, Japanese ends up with far fewer distinct syllabic possibilities—so collisions (homophones) are common.
If kana are mora-based, is that why Japanese is often described as mora-timed?

The writing system reflects mora structure, but the timing claim is about phonetics/phonology rather than orthography. Japanese rhythm is often described as mora-timed because morae tend to have relatively equal duration in speech (with many caveats). Kana are essentially a moraic orthography: each kana corresponds roughly to one mora (e.g., か = /ka/). Special morae include: - ん (moraic nasal) - っ (gemination marker) - Long vowels (often represented as two morae in hiragana, and as ー in katakana but still moraically long) So kana align well with how Japanese speakers segment speech. That makes kana excellent for representing pronunciation and for teaching reading.
If I wrote Japanese in roman letters (romaji), would that solve the problem? Lots of languages use alphabets and manage ambiguity.

Romaji “works” for transcription, and it’s used in specific contexts (input methods, passports, some signage, linguistics). But as a primary writing system for Japanese, it doesn’t solve the homophone and segmentation issues; it can actually make them worse. Reasons: 1) Word boundary ambiguity: Japanese writing normally doesn’t use spaces consistently. If you write everything in romaji without spaces, it’s hard to parse. If you add spaces, you have to decide where words begin/end—nontrivial in Japanese because particles attach, compounds form, and morphological boundaries can be ambiguous. 2) Homophones remain: kōshō is still “kōshō” in romaji. You might add diacritics for long vowels, but that doesn’t distinguish meanings. 3) Visual density/recognition: Kanji are visually distinct and allow rapid scanning. Romaji strings are longer and can be more uniform-looking. 4) Morphology: Kana/kanji mixture signals inflection boundaries. Romaji could do it with hyphens or apostrophes, but then you’re effectively inventing conventions that replicate what the mixed script already does naturally. So alphabets can handle ambiguity in languages with richer phonological distinctions and clearer word boundaries. Japanese’s particular combination makes logographs genuinely useful.
But Chinese uses mostly characters without kana. Why does Japanese need kana if Chinese doesn’t?

Because Japanese grammar is structurally very different from Chinese. Chinese (Mandarin, etc.) is largely analytic: grammatical relationships are often expressed via word order and function words, with relatively little inflection. Japanese is agglutinative: verbs and adjectives inflect heavily, and particles mark grammatical roles. If Japanese were written only with kanji, you’d need a way to represent inflectional endings and particles. Historically, classical Japanese used man’yōgana (kanji used phonetically) to represent Japanese sounds. Kana emerged from simplifying that system. In other words, kana are the streamlined solution for writing Japanese morphology and native phonology. Chinese does have phonetic annotation systems (pinyin, zhuyin) but those are supplemental. Japanese ended up integrating its phonetic system into normal orthography, because it’s essential for grammar.
You mentioned man’yōgana. Can you explain how that led to hiragana and katakana?

Man’yōgana was an early method of writing Japanese using Chinese characters purely for their sound values, not their meanings. Multiple kanji could represent the same Japanese syllable, because scribes chose characters based on approximate pronunciation. Over time, two simplifications happened: - Hiragana: developed from cursive, flowing writing of entire man’yōgana characters. A complex character was written quickly and stylized until it became a simpler symbol representing a mora. - Katakana: developed from taking a fragment (a component) of a man’yōgana character as a shorthand annotation. This produced more angular, modular shapes. So both kana systems trace back to kanji used phonetically, but they evolved along different scribal practices: cursive whole-character simplification vs component-based shorthand.
So hiragana and katakana represent the same sounds. Are there any pronunciation differences between them?

In standard modern Japanese, no inherent pronunciation difference: か and カ are both /ka/. Differences are in usage and sometimes in conventions for representing foreign sounds. Katakana is used more for approximating foreign phonemes using combinations and diacritics: - ティ (ti), ディ (di), ファ (fa), フィ (fi), ヴァ (va) with ヴ. Hiragana can also represent these, but katakana conventions dominate for loanwords. There are also stylistic uses: katakana can imply emphasis, mechanical tone, or even “foreignness”/technical flavor, but that’s a semantic-pragmatic layer, not phonetic.
How does kanji reading work then? If kanji encode meaning, how do you know how to pronounce them?

This is one of the complexities of Japanese literacy: kanji often have multiple readings. Broadly: - On’yomi: readings derived from Chinese pronunciations at different historical periods and regions. - Kun’yomi: native Japanese readings assigned to kanji based on meaning. For example, 生 can be read as: - セイ (on) in 学生 (がくせい) - しょう (on) in 一生 (いっしょう) - い(きる) (kun) in 生きる - う(まれる) (kun) in 生まれる - なま (kun) in 生 (なま) Which reading applies depends on the word (lexicalized compounds, native stems with okurigana, etc.). Kana (okurigana) often clarifies the reading and the grammatical form: 書く, 書きます, 書いた.
Okurigana is the hiragana after kanji, right? What’s its role beyond showing inflection?

Yes—okurigana are kana suffixes attached to kanji stems. They do several things: 1) Inflection marking: Verb/adjective endings are written in hiragana: 食べる, 高かった. 2) Disambiguation of readings: Many kanji could correspond to multiple native verbs/adjectives; okurigana helps specify which lexeme is intended. Example: 上げる (あげる) vs 上がる (あがる) share the kanji 上 but differ in okurigana and meaning. 3) Morphological transparency: It indicates where the kanji root ends and the inflectional material begins, aiding parsing. 4) Standardization: For some words, the choice of how much to write in kanji vs kana is standardized (though there is variation), and okurigana rules help keep that consistent.
Could Japanese reduce the number of kanji and rely more on kana while keeping some kanji for disambiguation?

That’s essentially what modern Japanese already does compared to older stages. There is a standardized set of commonly used kanji (jōyō kanji), and many rare characters are avoided in general publications. There’s also a list of kanji for names (jinmeiyō). In practice, writers choose: - Kanji for common content words (nouns, stems). - Kana for function words and inflections. - Kana for words with obscure kanji or where kana is conventional (e.g., きれい often written in kana or mixed forms; or こと, もの in many contexts). So the system has already evolved toward a balance: enough kanji to disambiguate and structure text, enough kana to keep it readable and writable.
What about readability studies? Do we know if mixed script is faster to read than kana-only?

There have been psycholinguistic studies suggesting that mixed kanji-kana text can be read more efficiently by proficient readers than kana-only text, primarily because kanji provide semantic and morphological cues and reduce ambiguity. Eye-tracking studies often show different fixation patterns: kanji can be processed as larger informational units. However, “faster” depends on reader proficiency and text type. For beginners, kana-only can be easier because it avoids unknown kanji. For skilled readers, kana-only can become slow due to parsing burden. Also, the advantage of kanji is larger in contexts with many homophones or dense technical vocabulary.
How do children learn then? Do they start with hiragana and then add kanji?

Typically, yes. Hiragana is taught first because it’s a complete phonographic system for Japanese. Katakana follows. Kanji are introduced gradually over years, starting with simpler, high-frequency characters. This progression mirrors functional necessity: - With hiragana, children can write any Japanese word phonetically. - Adding katakana enables reading loanwords and certain conventions. - Adding kanji increases reading efficiency and access to adult texts. The staged learning also reflects cognitive load: kana are finite and systematic; kanji require memorization of forms and multiple readings.
If kana are systematic, why do people still make mistakes with them? Is it just handwriting?

With kana, mistakes are less about “which symbol corresponds to which sound” (once learned) and more about: - Orthographic conventions: choice of kana for certain particles (は pronounced /wa/ as a topic marker; へ pronounced /e/; を pronounced /o/). These are historically motivated spellings that must be learned. - Long vowels in loanwords: katakana long vowel mark ー and how to represent long vowels can be tricky (e.g., コーヒー vs こおひい isn’t used; native long vowels have patterns like おう vs おお). - Small kana usage: ゃゅょ, っ, and in katakana small vowels (ァィゥェォ) for foreign sounds. - Handwriting legibility: distinguishing ぬ/め, わ/ね, る/ろ, シ/ツ, ソ/ン, etc., especially in certain fonts or quick writing. So kana are systematic, but the orthography includes conventions that aren’t purely phonetic.
That particle thing seems like it undermines the “efficient” argument. Why keep は as /wa/?

It’s a good example of how writing systems preserve historical layers. The topic marker は was historically closer to /pa/ and then /ha/ and eventually /wa/ in that grammatical position due to sound change, but the spelling stayed. Similarly, へ as /e/ and を as /o/. Keeping these spellings has a few consequences: - It provides morphological consistency: the particle is always written the same way regardless of pronunciation variation in fast speech. - It maintains continuity with older texts and conventions. Is it “efficient”? For learners, it’s an extra rule. For proficient readers, it’s automatic and can even help parsing because particles stand out as fixed spellings.
If kanji are so helpful, why do some words often get written in hiragana even when they have kanji?

Several reasons: 1) Kanji is rare/obscure: Some words have kanji that many readers don’t know, so kana is clearer. Example: たぶん (多分) is common in kanji, but something like きつね (狐) is often kana in casual contexts. 2) Nuance and softness: Hiragana can feel lighter or more neutral; kanji can feel stiff or overly formal. Writers choose script to set tone. 3) Function words and grammaticalized items: Words like こと, もの, ところ, ため often appear in hiragana when used in abstract grammatical senses, even though they have kanji (事, 物, 所, 為). Script choice can signal whether the word is concrete or grammatical. 4) Avoiding visual clutter: Too many kanji can make a sentence look heavy. Writers sometimes balance kanji density. So script choice isn’t purely about necessity; it’s also about style and reader processing.
That’s interesting: script choice as nuance. Does katakana have similar nuance beyond loanwords?

Yes. Katakana can signal: - Emphasis: like ALL CAPS or italics. Example: それはナイ. - A clinical/technical tone: especially in manuals or scientific writing. - Onomatopoeia and sound symbolism: which can feel vivid. - A detached or “objective label” feeling: writing certain common words in katakana can make them seem like terms rather than everyday words (e.g., コトバ for “word” in a meta-linguistic context). Because katakana is visually sharper and less frequent than hiragana in ordinary text, it stands out and carries pragmatic weight.
You said earlier that Japanese doesn’t use spaces consistently. Why not just add spaces and use kana or romaji? Wouldn’t that fix parsing?

Adding spaces would help segmentation, but it wouldn’t fully address homophones and semantic density. Also, deciding on spacing rules in Japanese is tricky because: - Compounds: Japanese forms long noun compounds. Should you write 経済成長率 as one unit or multiple? In kana/romaji with spaces, you’d need consistent conventions. - Particles: Should particles be separated (like “watashi wa”) or attached? Both are plausible. - Inflection: Verb endings attach. Do you separate stem and ending? That would be weird for native intuition. Japanese orthography evolved without spaces partly because mixed script already provides segmentation cues: kanji clusters for content, hiragana for grammar. In a kana-only or romaji-only system, spaces become more necessary, but implementing them would be a major orthographic reform with new ambiguities and style debates.
Could you show an example sentence and how it looks in different scripts?

Sure. Take a simple sentence: Mixed (typical): 私は昨日図書館で本を読んだ。 Kana-only: わたしはきのうとしょかんでほんをよんだ。 Romaji (no spaces): watashihakinoutoshokandehonoyonda Romaji (with spaces): watashi wa kinō toshokan de hon o yonda Notice: - Mixed script shows content words as kanji blocks: 私, 昨日, 図書館, 本, 読. - Kana-only is readable but more uniform; you rely more on context to segment. - Romaji without spaces is hard to parse. - Romaji with spaces helps segmentation but you still lose the semantic anchors from kanji, and you introduce decisions about spacing and long vowels (kinō vs kinuo, etc.).
In kana-only, “ほん” could be “本” or “奔” or something else, right?

Exactly. ほん could correspond to 本 (book), 盆 (tray/festival), 凡 (ordinary) in compounds, 奔 (dash), and more, depending on context. In everyday sentences, context usually resolves it, but as vocabulary grows and sentences get denser, the burden increases. Kanji also helps with proper nouns and technical terms where context might not be enough.
How does this compare to English, which has many homophones too?

English has homophones (two/too/to, right/write, etc.), but a few differences reduce the impact: - English has a much larger phoneme inventory and far more possible syllables due to consonant clusters, so homophone density is lower relative to vocabulary size. - English uses spaces obligatorily, giving strong segmentation cues. - English orthography is morphophonemic and irregular; spelling already encodes some distinctions even when pronunciation merges (e.g., right/write). That’s somewhat analogous to kanji disambiguation, but spread across alphabetic spelling conventions. Japanese kana are highly phonographic; they don’t preserve those distinctions. So kana-only is like a perfectly phonetic spelling system in a language with many homophones: it maximizes collisions.
Wait, English spelling disambiguates homophones, but it’s still alphabetic. So Japanese could just adopt a non-phonetic alphabetic spelling to disambiguate too, no?

In principle, yes: you could design an orthography that uses an alphabet but preserves etymological distinctions, or introduces arbitrary spellings to separate homophones. That’s essentially what English ended up with historically. But for Japanese, that would mean: - Creating a large set of conventional spellings not predictable from pronunciation, undermining the simplicity benefit of an alphabet. - Standardizing spellings for thousands of words and compounds, including Sino-Japanese morphemes, native words, and loanwords. - Dealing with multiple readings and morphological alternations. Kanji already serve that function and do it in a semantically grounded way. An arbitrary alphabetic disambiguation system would be a major, arguably less transparent, reform.
How about using furigana everywhere? Like keep kanji but always add hiragana reading, so pronunciation isn’t ambiguous.

That’s a real practice in certain contexts (children’s books, learner materials, some genres). Furigana (small kana above/beside kanji) provides pronunciation. If used everywhere, it would make reading accessible regardless of kanji knowledge, but it increases visual clutter and text length. In general adult writing, furigana is used selectively: - For rare kanji - For names - For stylistic effects (giving a nonstandard reading) - For technical terms So furigana can bridge the gap, but it’s not typically used universally because proficient readers don’t need it and it reduces typographic efficiency.
Nonstandard reading? Like giving kanji a different pronunciation on purpose?

Yes. Writers sometimes use kanji for meaning but attach furigana that indicates a different reading, often for stylistic nuance, metaphor, or to introduce a term while hinting at another concept. For example, using a kanji compound with a certain meaning but giving it a loanword reading in katakana via furigana. This creates a layered effect: semantic content from kanji, phonetic surface from the indicated reading. It’s a kind of controlled mismatch between orthography and phonology used for expressive writing.
That seems like a strong argument that the system is flexible, not just messy history.

Exactly. While the system arose historically, modern Japanese uses script choice as a functional toolkit. The “three scripts” are not redundant; they provide multiple channels: - Semantic channel (kanji) - Phonological/grammatical channel (hiragana) - Lexical/typographic channel (katakana) This multi-channel writing is powerful for a language with heavy compounding, many homophones, and rich inflection.
Can we talk more about how compounds work with kanji? Why are compounds such a big deal?

Sino-Japanese vocabulary is heavily compound-based. Many abstract nouns are formed by combining two (or more) kanji morphemes, each with relatively stable on’yomi. Examples: - 情報 (information) - 経済 (economy) - 社会 (society) - 関係 (relation) - 政策 (policy) These compounds are concise in kanji and remain readable even when pronunciation overlaps with other compounds. If written in kana, many would look similar and require more effort to parse. Kanji also support productive compounding: you can often infer meaning of a new compound from its components, even if you haven’t seen the exact word before—similar to how English readers infer meaning from Greek/Latin roots, but with stronger visual segmentation.
But doesn’t that inference depend on knowing the kanji meanings, which is hard?

It does require kanji knowledge, but that knowledge pays off because it scales. Once you know common characters and their semantic ranges, you can guess unfamiliar compounds more effectively. Also, kanji learning is incremental: - High-frequency kanji appear constantly, reinforcing memory. - Many compounds reuse the same kanji, creating a network of associations. So while the initial learning curve is steep, the system offers long-term efficiency for advanced literacy.
How do multiple readings affect this? If a kanji has many readings, doesn’t that reduce predictability?

It reduces predictability of pronunciation from kanji alone, but in compounds the reading is often predictable because compounds typically use on’yomi. Native words often use kun’yomi with okurigana. So there’s a rough division: - Kanji compounds (especially two-kanji nouns): usually on’yomi. - Inflected verbs/adjectives: usually kun’yomi with okurigana. - There are exceptions (ateji, mixed readings, jukujikun), but the general pattern helps. Moreover, the writing system prioritizes readability and meaning in context. Even if you don’t know the exact reading, you may understand the meaning from the kanji, which can be enough in some contexts.
Ateji and jukujikun—what are those?

Two special phenomena: 1) Ateji (当て字): kanji used for their sound rather than meaning (or sometimes chosen for meaning in a loose way), often in older spellings or certain loanwords. Example: 寿司 (sushi) historically is ateji; the characters don’t literally mean sushi. Another: 珈琲 for coffee. Modern writing usually uses katakana for loanwords, reducing ateji use. 2) Jukujikun (熟字訓): multi-kanji compounds with a native Japanese reading that isn’t a straightforward combination of individual kanji readings. Example: 今日 read as きょう; 明日 read as あした/あす; 大人 read as おとな. The kanji represent meaning, but the reading is lexicalized. These show that the system is partly lexical and conventional, not fully compositional.
Those seem like counterexamples to the “kanji disambiguate” idea, because pronunciation can’t be derived.

They’re counterexamples to “kanji predict pronunciation,” but not to “kanji disambiguate meaning.” In fact, jukujikun often enhance meaning clarity: 今日 as きょう is unambiguous in kanji form compared to きょう in kana, which could be 今日, 教, 協, 京… depending on context. So kanji still help with semantic identification even when reading is irregular.
Are there cases where kana disambiguates something that kanji would make ambiguous?

Yes, in a different way. Kana can disambiguate grammatical function and sometimes lexical choice: - Some kanji are visually similar or have multiple possible characters for a word; kana avoids choosing the “wrong” kanji or an overly formal one. - Kana can clarify that a word is being used in a grammaticalized sense rather than its concrete kanji meaning. For example, こと vs 事: writing こと can signal an abstract nominalizer rather than the concrete “matter/thing” sense. - Some homographs exist in kanji: the same kanji sequence can correspond to multiple words/meanings. Kana (or okurigana) can help differentiate inflected forms and lexemes. So the system is complementary: kanji disambiguate among homophones; kana disambiguate among grammatical functions and soften lexical edges.
I’m wondering about efficiency in terms of typing. Isn’t it slower to type Japanese because you have to convert to kanji?

Input methods (IME) add a conversion step, but they also leverage the structure of the writing system. Typical typing: 1) You type phonetic input (kana or romaji) to produce a kana string. 2) The IME suggests kanji conversions based on context. 3) You select the correct candidate. This is efficient because: - You don’t need separate keys for thousands of kanji. - Predictive conversion uses language models and context. - You can choose kana-only when appropriate. There is some overhead, especially with ambiguous readings, but skilled typists develop habits and the IME often gets it right. Interestingly, the kana-then-kanji conversion mirrors the conceptual separation: phonology first, then semantic disambiguation.
Would a purely alphabetic Japanese eliminate conversion overhead?

It would eliminate kanji conversion, but you might reintroduce other overhead: - You’d likely need more explicit spacing/punctuation to avoid ambiguity. - You’d have longer strings to type and read. - If you adopted non-phonetic spellings to disambiguate, you’d have to memorize those, which adds cognitive overhead. So the trade-off isn’t straightforward. Current Japanese input is optimized for the existing orthography.
Let’s get more technical: what exactly counts as a “writing system”? Are hiragana and katakana separate systems or scripts?

In typological terms: - Kanji are logographs (morphograms), representing morphemes/meaning units. - Kana are syllabaries (more precisely moraic syllabaries), representing morae. Hiragana and katakana are two scripts (two graphic sets) that implement the same moraic mapping. So they’re separate scripts but not separate “types” of writing system. Japanese orthography is best described as a mixed system: morphographic + moraic. So when people say “three writing systems,” they often mean three scripts used in orthography.
If kana are moraic, do they ever represent just consonants like an alphabet?

Not in the core system. Kana represent morae, typically CV. There are exceptions: - ん represents a moraic nasal consonant. - っ represents gemination (a mora that signals a following consonant is doubled). But there aren’t standalone consonant letters like “k” or “t.” In katakana, there are extended combinations using small vowels to approximate consonant-vowel sequences not native to Japanese (ティ, トゥ, フォ), but still they’re built from mora-like chunks.
Does this moraic nature affect how Japanese borrows words?

Strongly. Loanwords are adapted to fit Japanese mora structure: - Consonant clusters are broken with vowels: “strike” → ストライク (su-to-ra-i-ku). - Final consonants often get a vowel: “desk” → デスク (de-su-ku). - /l/ and /r/ merge into /ɾ/; /v/ becomes /b/ or ヴ in newer loans; /θ/ becomes /s/ or /sh/ etc. Katakana provides a standardized way to represent these adapted pronunciations. This is another reason katakana is useful: it signals “this is a borrowed form with Japanese phonology applied.”
If loanwords are in katakana, are there native words in katakana too?

Yes, though less common, and usually for effect or convention: - Some animal and plant names are often in katakana in certain registers (field guides, labels). - Onomatopoeia is frequently katakana. - Brand-like styling: writing a native word in katakana can make it look like a label, a term, or something emphasized. So katakana isn’t exclusively for loanwords; it’s a script for “marked” vocabulary in a broad sense.
How do Japanese readers process mixed scripts cognitively? Do they switch modes?

There’s evidence that different scripts engage different processing routes: - Kanji recognition relies more on visual-orthographic and semantic processing, akin to word recognition in logographic systems. - Kana reading relies more on phonological decoding due to consistent sound mapping. Skilled readers integrate both seamlessly. Mixed script may reduce cognitive load because each script carries different information: kanji give immediate semantic cues; kana provide grammatical and phonological glue. This is somewhat like how English readers process content words vs function words, but Japanese makes it visually explicit via script.
Does the presence of kanji reduce the need for punctuation?

To some extent, yes. Kanji-kana mixture gives segmentation cues, which can reduce reliance on spaces. Japanese still uses punctuation (、。) and quotation marks, parentheses, etc., but the script mixture helps show boundaries that in alphabetic languages are often reinforced by spaces. That said, punctuation conventions differ by genre and style, and modern Japanese writing uses punctuation widely.
Could you explain more about “visual segmentation”? What exactly are the cues?

In mixed script text: - Kanji sequences often correspond to content morphemes: nouns, stems, compound parts. - Hiragana sequences often correspond to particles, auxiliary verbs, inflectional endings, and some native words. So the alternation itself creates a pattern. For instance: 彼は新しい計画を実行する。 You can visually pick out: - Content: 彼, 新, 計画, 実行 - Grammar: は, しい, を, する Even without spaces, the script boundaries act like implicit delimiters. This helps the eye group characters into units for parsing.
But sometimes long hiragana sequences happen too, right? Like in polite forms?

Yes. Polite and auxiliary constructions can create longer hiragana runs: - していませんでした - させられそうでした Also, some texts (children’s books, casual writing) may use more hiragana. In those cases, the segmentation advantage decreases, and readers rely more on syntactic expectations and particles. Still, even in those sequences, particles and common auxiliaries create recognizable patterns.
Is there a reason hiragana became associated with “native” and katakana with “foreign”? Or is that a modern convention?

It’s partly historical and partly modern standardization. Historically: - Hiragana was used in a wide range of native writing and became prominent in certain literary traditions. - Katakana was used in scholarly annotation and later in official/technical contexts. Modern conventions solidified with language planning and printing practices: - Loanwords increased in the modern era; katakana became the standard way to write them. - Hiragana remained the default for native grammatical elements. So the association is conventional, but it has historical roots in how the scripts were used in different domains.
Are there any proposals to abolish kanji? I’ve heard of script reforms.

There have been reform movements advocating kana-only or reduced-kanji usage, especially around modernization and literacy efforts. Some arguments included: - Simplifying education - Reducing learning burden - Improving accessibility However, complete abolition faces strong obstacles: - The functional benefits of kanji for disambiguation and readability - The massive corpus of existing writing - The role of kanji in names and technical vocabulary - Cultural and institutional inertia (though we’re not going into cultural connections; practically, it’s about continuity and standardization) The compromise approach—limiting to a standard set, simplifying forms (shinjitai vs kyūjitai), and using kana for many items—became the mainstream outcome.
You mentioned simplified forms. How does that interact with the three-script system?

Simplification primarily affects kanji. Post-reform simplified character forms (shinjitai) reduce stroke counts for some common kanji (e.g., 國 → 国, 體 → 体). Kana were already relatively simple and standardized. This interacts with the system by making the kanji component easier to learn and write, supporting the continued viability of mixed script. If kanji remained highly complex across the board, the cost-benefit balance might tilt more toward kana-only writing.
What about digital fonts and printing—did they shape script usage?

Yes, technology influences orthography. Printing standardized shapes and encouraged consistent usage. Typewriters and early computing had constraints: limited character sets made kanji difficult, leading to increased kana usage in some contexts and motivating encoding standards. Modern computing solved much of that with Unicode and IMEs, making kanji easy to input and display. That technological support reinforces the continued use of kanji. Katakana also benefited from typographic conventions: it’s clear and angular, making it suitable for headings, labels, and technical text.
Japanese seems like it uses scripts almost like formatting. Is that fair?

Largely, yes. Scripts in Japanese are partly orthographic and partly typographic. Katakana functions somewhat like italics/bold/ALL CAPS in alphabetic writing, but with a standardized linguistic role (loanwords, etc.). Kanji provide semantic compression. Hiragana provides grammatical baseline. So script choice is a built-in formatting layer that also carries linguistic information.
Does this cause problems for dyslexia or reading disorders?

Reading disorders can manifest differently depending on the writing system. In a mixed system, difficulties might target: - Visual-orthographic recognition (kanji) - Phonological decoding (kana) - Integration of both Some learners may find kana easier due to regularity, while kanji can be challenging due to visual complexity and memory demands. On the other hand, kanji’s semantic cues can sometimes support comprehension once recognized. Research suggests that the profile of reading difficulties differs from alphabetic languages, but it’s complex and depends on the individual and educational context.
I’m curious about the claim that kanji reduce character count. Is that actually a big deal in practice?

Often, yes. Kanji can compress multi-mora words into fewer symbols. Example: - しょうひしゃ (shōhisha) → 消費者 (3 characters) - じょうほうしょり (jōhō shori) → 情報処理 (4 characters) Kana versions can be much longer. This matters for: - Visual scanning and layout - Short labels and headings - Reducing line length and aiding chunking However, compression alone isn’t always beneficial—very dense kanji text can look intimidating, and some kana is necessary for grammar. The advantage is in balanced mixture.
But if kanji are compact, why not also use them for loanwords? Could we create kanji for them?

Historically, some loanwords were written with ateji (kanji chosen for sound) or with semantic calques (translating meaning). But modern practice uses katakana because: - It clearly marks the word as a borrowed item and signals approximate pronunciation. - Creating new kanji or forcing existing ones for loanwords would be hard to standardize and would reintroduce reading ambiguity. - Katakana is flexible for representing foreign sound patterns (within Japanese constraints). So katakana is essentially the “loanword channel,” avoiding the complexity of inventing new morphograms.
Is katakana ever used for native grammatical elements?

Rarely in standard writing, but it can be done for stylistic reasons (to sound robotic, to emphasize, to mimic signage). For example, writing particles or endings in katakana would look unusual and would be interpreted as deliberate stylistic marking. In standard orthography, grammatical elements are overwhelmingly hiragana.
What about mixing scripts inside one word?

It happens commonly: - Kanji + hiragana: typical for verbs/adjectives (書いた, 高い) and many nouns with suffixes. - Kanji + katakana: can occur in technical terms or to mark part of a compound as a loanword. - Katakana + hiragana: less common but can appear in playful writing or to attach native suffixes to loanwords (e.g., サボり from サボる; though that’s more katakana + hiragana endings). - Kanji + kana + kanji: common in longer expressions. This flexibility is part of how Japanese represents morphology and lexical origin.
You mentioned サボる. That’s a loanword that becomes a verb, right? How does the writing system handle that?

Exactly. Loanwords often become verbs by attaching する or by deriving a verb with -る: - コピーする (to copy) - ダウンロードする (to download) - サボる (to slack off; from French sabotage historically) Orthographically, the borrowed base is usually katakana, and the native verb morphology is hiragana: - コピーした - ダウンロードしています - サボった So scripts map nicely onto morphological layers: foreign lexical base (katakana) + native inflection (hiragana). Kanji can also appear if the verb is Sino-Japanese (勉強する) or native (食べる).
That makes the system sound almost engineered.

It’s an emergent design: historical accident plus functional stabilization. Over time, conventions that improved readability and expressiveness tended to persist, especially once standardized education and printing reinforced them.
If we imagine an alternative history where Japanese adopted an alphabet early, what would be the likely consequences?

Hypothetically, an alphabetic Japanese would have had to address: - Homophone density: either tolerate it, add spaces, or create etymological spellings. - Morphological marking: perhaps use hyphens or standardized affixes. - Borrowing: represent foreign sounds as best as possible. It might end up resembling something like: - A consistent phonemic spelling with many spaces and perhaps diacritics for pitch accent or vowel length, or - A morphologically-based spelling that distinguishes Sino-Japanese roots with special spellings. But those solutions would be new conventions to learn. The current system offloads much of that onto kanji and script alternation.
You brought up pitch accent. Does writing represent pitch accent at all?

Standard Japanese orthography does not mark pitch accent. It’s learned through exposure. In dictionaries and learning materials, pitch accent can be marked with special notation, but it’s not part of everyday writing. This is another area where a hypothetical “more phonetic” writing system could encode more pronunciation detail, but that would increase complexity and likely not be adopted widely because context usually suffices for comprehension.
If pitch accent isn’t written, does kanji help with it indirectly?

Indirectly, yes. Kanji distinguishes words that might be homophones in kana, and those words may also differ in pitch accent patterns. By identifying the intended word via kanji, you can choose the correct accent if you know it. But for many speakers, accent variation exists, and not all distinctions are critical for comprehension. Kanji primarily helps with lexical identity, which can correlate with accent patterns.
How do dictionaries handle multiple readings? Is there a standard ordering?

Dictionaries typically list: - The kanji spelling(s) - Kana reading(s) - Part of speech and meanings - For verbs/adjectives, the dictionary form reading and sometimes conjugation class - On/kun readings for individual kanji in kanji dictionaries Ordering can be by frequency or conventional “main” reading. For kanji entries, on’yomi often listed first, then kun’yomi, but conventions vary. Modern learner dictionaries prioritize the reading used in the target word.
Coming back to efficiency: is the three-script system actually “more efficient” overall, or just different?

For proficient adult readers, mixed script is often efficient in reading speed and comprehension because it reduces ambiguity and improves chunking. For writing/learning, it has higher upfront cost because you must learn kanji. So efficiency depends on what you measure: - Acquisition efficiency: kana-only or alphabetic would be easier initially. - Mature literacy efficiency: mixed script can be faster and clearer for dense text. Japanese orthography seems optimized for mature literacy in a language with high homophony and agglutinative morphology, at the expense of a longer learning curve.
Are there comparable mixed systems elsewhere?

A few comparisons: - Korean historically used mixed Hanja (Chinese characters) + Hangul; modern Korean is mostly Hangul, but Hanja appears sometimes for disambiguation. - Vietnamese used chữ Nôm historically (logographs) before adopting Latin alphabet. - English uses alphabet but incorporates logographic-like elements (numerals, symbols) and morphophonemic spelling. But Japanese is unusual in routinely using two phonetic scripts plus logographs in everyday writing.
Korean moved away from Hanja. Does that suggest Japanese could too?

It suggests it’s possible, but the linguistic and policy contexts differ. Korean has a larger syllable inventory and Hangul with spaces provides segmentation. Also, modern Korean orthography has conventions that help. Japanese could theoretically reduce kanji further, and there has been some reduction, but eliminating kanji entirely would likely increase ambiguity and reduce readability for many adult texts unless compensated by spaces and/or new spelling conventions. So “could” is not the same as “would be beneficial.” The equilibrium Japanese has reached seems stable because it balances multiple needs.
If someone is learning Japanese, should they focus on kana first, then kanji, or learn kanji early?

Kana first is essential because it’s the base for pronunciation and grammar. But introducing kanji relatively early is beneficial because: - It helps vocabulary retention by adding meaning cues. - It prevents overreliance on romaji and kana-only reading, which can become a crutch. - It aligns with how real texts look. A common approach: master hiragana quickly, learn basic katakana, then start kanji alongside vocabulary in context, focusing on common characters and words rather than isolated readings.
That makes sense. But if kanji have multiple readings, should learners memorize readings or words?

Prioritize words. Memorizing a list of on/kun readings without lexical context is inefficient. Better: - Learn kanji with a few high-frequency words that use it. - Note patterns: compounds often use on’yomi; okurigana words use kun’yomi. - Gradually expand. Over time, you internalize readings as part of vocabulary. Kanji knowledge becomes a network of form-meaning mappings rather than a table of readings.
Is there any logic to which on’yomi reading is used in a compound, given there can be multiple on’yomi too?

Sometimes, but often it’s lexicalized. Multiple on’yomi reflect different borrowing periods (Go-on, Kan-on, Tō-on, etc.). Which one appears in a given compound depends on historical formation and convention. There are patterns: - Certain kanji prefer one on’yomi in common compounds. - Some readings appear in specific semantic fields or in certain compound positions. But predicting with certainty is hard; it’s best learned through vocabulary exposure.
So the writing system is partly historical layers and partly functional.

Exactly. Think of it as: - Historical layering: multiple readings, irregular compounds, particle spellings. - Functional optimization: script division of labor, disambiguation, segmentation, typographic signaling. Japanese orthography is a living compromise that accumulated features and then stabilized around what works for readers and writers.
I want to test the ambiguity claim. Give me a kana-only phrase that’s genuinely ambiguous in a way kanji would fix.

Here’s a classic style of example: Kana-only: かみをきった This could be: - 髪を切った (cut hair) - 紙を切った (cut paper) - 神を斬った (slashed a god) — less likely but possible in fiction In normal context, “cut hair” is most plausible, but without context it’s ambiguous. Kanji immediately resolves it. Another: Kana-only: はしをわたる - 橋を渡る (cross a bridge) - 箸を渡る (pass chopsticks) — odd but possible in some context - 端を渡る (go across the edge) Kanji clarifies.
Those are good. But in English, “I cut it” could mean cut hair, paper, etc. We rely on context too.

True, but Japanese ambiguity is often at the lexical identity level even when the surrounding syntax is identical, and the density of collisions is higher. English ambiguity often involves unspecified objects or pronouns; Japanese kana-only ambiguity can occur even when the object is explicitly stated (かみ), because that object itself is ambiguous. Kanji shifts some burden from context to orthography.
Are there cases where kanji create ambiguity because multiple kanji share the same form?

Kanji can create homographs if the same character sequence corresponds to multiple meanings or readings. For example: - 生物 can be せいぶつ (living thing) or なまもの (raw food) depending on context (though なまもの is often written 生もの). - 今日 can be きょう; in some contexts こんにち (as in 今日のところ) though less common. But generally, kanji reduce ambiguity more than they create it, and when they do create it, context resolves it. Kana or okurigana choices often mitigate.
How do proper names fit into this? Names seem like a nightmare.

Names are indeed complex: - Given names and surnames can use uncommon kanji. - Readings can be nonstandard. - The same name can have multiple possible readings. Orthographically, kanji are used for names, but pronunciation may require furigana in contexts like school rosters, official forms, or introductions. This is one area where the semantic/visual advantage of kanji (distinct identity) is prioritized even though it increases reading unpredictability.
That’s almost the opposite of efficiency.

For pronunciation, yes. For identity and differentiation, kanji are useful: many names would collapse in kana. Kanji allow distinct written forms even when names are pronounced similarly. So the “efficiency” goal shifts: it’s about unique labeling rather than ease of reading.
Bringing it back: if someone says “one alphabet would be more efficient,” how would you respond succinctly?

I’d say: For Japanese, a single phonetic alphabet would be easier to learn initially, but it would likely be less efficient for adult reading because Japanese has many homophones and complex morphology. The mixed system uses kanji to encode meaning and reduce ambiguity, hiragana to encode grammar and inflection, and katakana to label loanwords and emphasis—together improving readability and expressive control.
I’m satisfied with that, but I’d like to go deeper into the “logic.” Is there a formal way to describe what each script encodes?

You can describe it in terms of linguistic units: - Kanji: primarily morphographic—often correspond to content morphemes (roots) and encode lexical meaning. They also correlate with Sino-Japanese morphemes used in compounding. - Hiragana: primarily morphophonographic—encodes morae, used for grammatical morphemes (particles, auxiliaries), inflectional endings, and some lexical items (especially native or function-like words). It often marks morphosyntactic information (tense, politeness, etc.) via endings. - Katakana: also moraic phonography, but used to encode “marked lexicon”: loans, onomatopoeia, technical labels, emphasis. So it encodes a pragmatic/lexical feature: “this is not ordinary native lexicon” or “treat this as a label.” So you can model Japanese orthography as mapping different morpheme classes to different scripts, with exceptions.
Could you give a sentence and annotate which parts are which morpheme classes?

Example: 新しいアルゴリズムを試してみた。 - 新しい: 新 (kanji root meaning “new”) + しい (hiragana adjective ending) - アルゴリズム: katakana loanword noun - を: hiragana particle - 試して: 試 (kanji verb root “try/test”) + して (hiragana inflection/te-form) - みた: hiragana auxiliary verb 見る in grammaticalized sense “try doing” + past This shows: - Content roots in kanji - Loanword in katakana - Grammar and inflection in hiragana
Nice. How does this relate to information theory? Like entropy and redundancy?

Mixed script introduces redundancy across channels: phonological information is partly present (kana), semantic information is partly present (kanji), and script choice signals lexical class (katakana). Redundancy can increase robustness and reduce ambiguity, which can lower uncertainty during reading. From an information-theoretic perspective: - Kana-only maximizes phonological encoding but minimizes semantic disambiguation, increasing ambiguity (entropy) at the lexical level. - Kanji adds constraints, reducing uncertainty about word identity. - Script alternation adds structural cues, reducing uncertainty about morpheme boundaries and lexical class. So while learning cost is higher, mature reading may require fewer “bits” of contextual inference to decode the intended message.
That’s a cool framing. Does it mean Japanese is “more redundant” than English?

In some respects, Japanese orthography can be more redundant because it encodes multiple layers (meaning + sound + lexical class). English orthography also has redundancy (irregular spelling, morphological consistency), but it’s mostly within one script. Comparing redundancy across languages is tricky because: - Languages differ in morphology and phonology. - Orthographies encode different things (phonemes, morphemes, etymology). - Reading strategies differ. But yes, Japanese mixed script can be seen as a redundancy strategy that improves error tolerance and speed for skilled readers.
Error tolerance—like if you misread a kana, kanji might still give you meaning?

Exactly. Kanji can help you recover meaning even if pronunciation is uncertain. Conversely, kana endings can help you interpret grammatical function even if a kanji is unfamiliar, and furigana can fill gaps when needed. In noisy conditions (blurry print, partial knowledge), having multiple cues can support comprehension.
Final question: if you had to design an “optimal” Japanese orthography from scratch, would you keep three scripts?

If the goal is optimizing adult readability and maintaining clear distinctions among homophones while keeping grammar transparent, I’d keep a mixed system: a morphographic component for content morphemes and a phonographic component for inflection and function words. Whether you need two separate phonographic scripts (hiragana and katakana) depends on whether you value an extra typographic channel for lexical marking. Given how useful it is to visually flag loanwords and emphasis without extra punctuation, I’d likely keep two kana sets or an equivalent typographic distinction. So: the three-script setup is not the only possible solution, but it’s a very effective one for the constraints Japanese has.
